issue_title: Multi-line basic string trims empty lines
issue_body:
From spec:

> Multi-line basic strings are surrounded by three quotation marks on each side and allow newlines. A newline immediately following the opening delimiter will be trimmed. All other whitespace and newline characters remain intact.

Test toml:

```
str1 = """
Roses are red

Violets are blue"""
```

Result:

```
>>> toml.load('test.toml')['str1']
'Roses are red\nViolets are blue'
```

Expected result:

```
>>> toml.load('test.toml')['str1']
'Roses are red\n\nViolets are blue'
```

issue_title: Decoding for blocks is removed, when data ist changed
issue_body:
### Example

- Editing protocol with two encodings and CRC
- Set encoding for each block
- Notice, for some blocks the CRC does not match
- You go back to interpretation and adapt the signal paramters
- To check CRC, you need to set the decoding in Interpretation again
- Even worse: You need to reset the decoding for __all__ blocks of the signal

### Solutions
1. Save a list of defined decodings before reinterpreting the signal. However, if the new number of blocks does not match the old, this may have counterintuitive effects.
2. tbd.

issue_title: ipdb.set_trace() breaks inspect
issue_body:
I originally filed here: https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/8671

If a script attempts to use inspect.getfile() on a class that was defined in a script file after set_trace() is called an exception is raised. Here's the traceback and the demo script.

I discovered that ipython is replacing sys.modules['main'] at some point (didn't get as far as pinpointing an exact line). This object ipython puts in there doesn't have attributes inspect is looking for which leads to the exception. It seems ipython could clean up after itself when set_trace() exits or copy those attributes to the replaced item.

I'd really like to get a work around so I can make a local modification until this fix goes out.

Version info
Python 2.7.10

    pip freeze | grep ipython
    ipython==3.2.1
    pip freeze | grep ipdb
    ipdb==0.8.1

Traceback

    > /Users/username/folder/test.py(10)test()
          9         ipdb.set_trace()
    ---> 10         inspect.getfile(self.__class__)
         11
    ipdb> c
    E
    ======================================================================
    ERROR: test (__main__.Test)
    ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "test_test.py", line 10, in test
        inspect.getfile(self.__class__)
      File "/Users/username/homebrew/Cellar/python/2.7.10_2/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/inspect.py", line 408, in getfile
    raise TypeError('{!r} is a built-in class'.format(object))
    TypeError: <module '__main__' (built-in)> is a built-in class
    ----------------------------------------------------------------------
    Ran 1 test in 1.268s

    FAILED (errors=1)

Script for reproduction

    import inspect
    import unittest
    import ipdb

    class Test(unittest.TestCase):
        def test(self):
            inspect.getfile(self.__class__)
            ipdb.set_trace()
            inspect.getfile(self.__class__)

    def main():
        unittest.main()

    if __name__ == '__main__':
        main()

issue_title: Discussion: optionally calling sleep/after on no retry
issue_body:
Today tenacity only invokes```sleep``` or ```after``` if a called function will retry [1].

What are your thoughts on making these configurable to run even if there's no retry?


Potential use cases might be introducing delay on certain conditions and/or invoking after hooks. For example [2].

Or even something as simple as:
```py
# introduce a random sleep when a timeout occurs and log it
@retry(retry= retry_if_exception_type(TimeoutErr),  wait= wait_random(), retry=retry_none(), after=log_timeout)
def might_raise_timeout(...):
```

While this usage may teeter the definition of retrying, it does provide some additional use cases with minimal changes to tenacity.


[1] https://github.com/jd/tenacity/blob/master/tenacity/__init__.py#L187
[2] https://github.com/openstack/neutron/blob/master/neutron/common/rpc.py#L128

issue_title: vext logger can cause unexpected behaviour in other modules which use logging
issue_body:
I ran into this issue whilst working on some code which requires vext.pyqt4. Every time I install vext.pyqt4, all my logging output  disappears. I finally managed to track down the problem and figured that it might be possible to fix the problem/help someone else who runs into this.

vext.pyqt4 seems to instantiate an environment-wide logger. By this, I mean that there will be an active logger prior to any imports. This can be seen by starting a python shell and running:

```
import logging
print logging.Logger.manager.loggerDict
```

The problem with this is that it means the vext logger configures the root logger and sets its log level to WARNING. Thus, any new loggers will adhere to the log level of the root logger and most logging will be suppressed. It is obviously possible to change the log level on the root logger, but that can cause vext to clutter up existing logging. I chose to solve this (perhaps not the best approach, but I was in need of an easy fix) by putting the following right at the top of my code:

```
if 'vext' in logging.Logger.manager.loggerDict.keys():
    for handler in logging.root.handlers:
        logging.root.removeHandler(handler)
    logging.getLogger('vext').setLevel(logging.WARNING)
```

This allows my code to set up the logger as expected while still allowing vext to log warning level messages. Hope this is vaguely helpful.

issue_title: Question/Discussion: Performance of subtraction of pint is slower than the naive approach
issue_body:
I am profiling my code (which uses pint) and I noticed that some array operations were surprisingly slow (like array subtraction with different units) compared to manual unit scaling. Below is an example that demonstrates this.

Question: Why is the pint approach slower? Is there something about pint which I missed which might speed up the process?

```python
class test_pint:
    def __init__(self, a, b):
        self.a = a
        self.b = b

    def test(self):
        self.a -= self.b

        return self.a

class test_pint_manual:
    def __init__(self, a, b):
        self.a = a
        self.b = b

    def test(self):
        units_b = Quantity(1., self.b.units)
        scale = units_b.to(self.a.units).magnitude
        _arr = self.b.magnitude
        _arr *= scale
        _arr2 = self.a.magnitude
        _arr2 -= _arr

        return self.a
```

Testing like this:

    a = np.random.rand(100, 100, 100)
    b = np.random.rand(100, 100, 100)
    arr1 = Quantity(np.copy(a), 'foot/second')
    arr2 = Quantity(np.copy(b), 'kg/g*m/year')
    arr3 = Quantity(np.copy(a), 'foot/second')
    arr4 = Quantity(np.copy(b), 'kg/g*m/year')
    t_auto = test_pint(arr1, arr2)
    t_manual = test_pint_manual(arr3, arr4)
    %timeit t_auto.test()
    %timeit t_manual.test()
    np.testing.assert_allclose(t_auto.test(), t_manual.test())

    6.51 ms ± 185 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
    2.66 ms ± 12.2 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)

So the manual solution is about 2x faster than the pint solution. This is also true for larger arrays (tested with 1000**3). But I would've expected pint to do exactly what I was doing, so what is pint doing differently?

issue_title: IPv6 doesn't handle ESP packets sanely
issue_body:
_From [nux...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/106810974862880856166/) on September 12, 2014 12:26:59_

Under IPv6, ESP packets are "handled" as an Extension Heaeder and dpkt raises a NotImplemented Exception.


Since the next-hdr field of an ESP header refers to the encrypted payload, I don't think there is really much that could be done here. Firing an exception is a bit extreme, I think it is better to just not consider this an extension and let the parser stop there.


Attached is a diff that removes ESP from the list of extension headers.

**Attachment:** [ip6-esp.diff](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=132)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=132_

issue_title: Suspicious alignments of regions with Ns stretches
issue_body:
We noticed suspicious alignments of the [attached scaffolded fragment](https://github.com/lh3/minimap2/files/1951322/NM_064705.3_score0.25_extractedTargets.fa.zip) against the C. elegans reference genome (downloaded from here: ftp://ftp.ensemblgenomes.org/pub/metazoa/release-39/fasta/caenorhabditis_elegans/dna/Caenorhabditis_elegans.WBcel235.dna.toplevel.fa.gz).

The running command was: 
`./minimap2 -c -x asm5 -B5 -r 50 --no-long-join -N 50 -s 65 -z 200 --mask-level 0.9 -f 200 --cs Caenorhabditis_elegans.WBcel235.dna.toplevel.fa scaffold.fa`

The fragment includes two stretches of 200 Ns. We expect minimap2 to break the alignment at this stretches since we use `-r 50` and 200 >> 50. However, we get:

issue_title: Correct handling of Unicode in py2
issue_body:
I'm not a py3 coder, so this is regarding py2.

When trying to include a _.csv_ file which is utf-8 encoded, the filter fails miserably as panflute expects unicode data (it calls `text.encode('utf-8')` on line 338 of _panflute/tools.py_)

You are using the built in `csv` module, which clearly states in the docs, that "The csv module doesn’t directly support reading and writing Unicode [...]".   Aka, you should convert the read data to unicode before returning it as `raw_table_list` in `read_data()` at line 197 of _pantable.py_.

Or perhaps more preferrable, just use the [unicodecsv](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/unicodecsv/0.9.0) module, which claims to be a "drop-in replacement for Python 2’s csv module which supports unicode strings without a hassle."

I tested unicodecsv in a local checkout, and it seems to work flawlessly.  I just added `import unicodecsv as csv`, not caring about anything than py2 :)

issue_title: Strange behavior of Mask on grouped df
issue_body:
Mask doesn't seem to work correctly on grouped dataframes. Consider the three operations below.

```
diamonds >> mask(X.cut == 'Ideal') >> groupby(X.color)  # works as expected
diamonds >> groupby(X.color) >> mask(X.cut == 'Ideal')  # doesn't work correctly (strange behavior)
diamonds >> mask(X.cut == 'Ideal')                                    # works as expected
```

In the first example, the mask is applied before the grouping, so it behaves as expected.
In the second example, the grouping is applied before the mask. The returned dataframe includes cases where X.cut != 'Ideal', and returns about 2500 rows. I'm not sure what causes the returned rows to be returned.
In the third example, there is no grouping, and the data behaves as expected (returns the same result as the first example.

There are use cases where you might want to use grouping and mask together (for example, to return the min for each group, you might want to do something like

```
diamonds >> groupby(X.color) >> mask(X.x == X.x.min())        (1)
diamonds >> mask(X.x == X.x.min())                                          (2)
```
For (1), it returns a single row, where x is not a min for any group, but (2) behaves as expected.

issue_title: load OpenLDAP settings automatically
issue_body:
I suspect a lot of people using LDAP already have things like the URI, search base, etc. defined in `~/.ldaprc` or something similar. It would be nice if `ldap3` could use these settings automatically rather than requiring them to be repeated in code.

I’d be happy to add this ability via pull request, but I want to make sure you’re open to the idea first.

FYI, it would follow the same rules as OpenLDAP and check the following places for settings (from lowest to highest precedence):

  * System-wide `ldap.conf` (found in `/etc` or `/usr/local/etc`)
  * The user’s `~/.ldaprc`
  * A file named `ldaprc` in the current directory
  * Environment variables that have names beginning with “LDAP”

Of course, in the case of this library, settings defined in code would be respected above all the other sources.

issue_title: GMSH: physical and elementary tags in the `$Elements` section
issue_body:
This is a piece of the Gmsh online docs: http://gmsh.info/doc/texinfo/gmsh.html#MSH-ASCII-file-format

_number-of-tags_
gives the number of integer tags that follow for the n-th element. By default, the first tag is the number of the physical entity to which the element belongs; the second is the number of the elementary geometrical entity to which the element belongs; the third is the number of mesh partitions to which the element belongs, followed by the partition ids (negative partition ids indicate ghost cells). A zero tag is equivalent to no tag. Gmsh and most codes using the MSH 2 format require at least the first two tags (physical and elementary tags).

Please note the last sentence. At some point you modified the writer to not output the physical and elementary tags in the `$Elements` section. However, PETSc https://www.mcs.anl.gov/petsc/ expects these tags right there in the `$Elements` section. I beg you to revert this change, at this point `meshio` is basically unusable for me.

issue_title: Long-polling-requests likely end in a request timeout
issue_body:
The keep-alive-noops for long-polling request are not sent every 15sec anymore.

The timeout is triggered in the current version after 30sec, for missing two noops in a row.
https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/channel.py#L35..L37
https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/blob/master/hangups/channel.py#L325..L327


I increased the logging and can report a new average of about 27 sec between the noops.
Missing one noop already tiggers a timeout then, which could happen very likely:
warnings from the last week: [hangups_channel_warn.txt](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/files/1054769/hangups_channel_warn.txt)

Meta to the captured traffic mentioned above:
noops: 449
newsessions: 48
duration: 3h01m14s
insec: 10874
avg = insec/(noops-newsessions) = 27.1s
*subtract the new sessions as a noop is sent right after opening a long-polling-request*
full log: [hangups_noops.txt](https://github.com/tdryer/hangups/files/1054767/hangups_noops.txt)

issue_title: AWS Route53 InvalidChangeBatch when adding a record type that already exists
issue_body: When authorising a wildcard certificate for Let's Encrypt, it tries to add two TXT records for the same subdomain. With Memset I can do this in `--manual-auth-hook`: ```bash lexicon "$PROVIDER" create "$CERTBOT_DOMAIN" TXT --name "_acme-challenge.$CERTBOT_DOMAIN" --content "$CERTBOT_VALIDATION" --ttl 300 ``` (Which is very useful - thanks!!) But with Route53 I get this error for the second record: ```xml SenderInvalidChangeBatchTried to create resource record set [name='_acme-challenge.xenial.djm.me.', type='TXT'] but it already exists259cf885-1f32-11e8-981c-cd2103792d25 ``` In the Route53 API I believe they have to be added as [a single record with multiple values](https://serverfault.com/a/616411/76878) instead of separate records, but I can't see a way to do that with Lexicon. I'm going to try using the [Certbot Route53 plugin](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/tree/master/certbot-dns-route53) for now, but it would be great if Lexicon could handle this. Thanks again!

issue_title: Reload router kills SSH session before I can manually close it down
issue_body:
Hey Kurt, firstly thanks for all your hard work.

I'm trying to upgrade a bunch of routers, iterating through a text file for IPs, changing the boot parameters, then issuing a write mem/reload.  I'm using the following, similar to your ASA upgrade example code:

```
output = sshConnection.send_command_expect('write mem')
output += sshConnection.send_command_timing('reload')
output += sshConnection.send_command('y')
print output

sshConnection.disconnect()
```

The issue comes that as soon as the router acknowledges the 'y' and begins the reload process, the script hangs, presumably because the router has killed the SSH session, so it can't get the prompt back that the command expects.  

It never advances to the 'print' statement, and never closes down the SSH connection, eventually failing with:

`IOError: Search pattern never detected in send_command_expect`

I need it to move onto the next lines in order to loop back through the list of IP addresses, and connect to the next router.  

Could you advise if there's a better way of handling the reload that will allow me to close the connection gracefully on my side and continue the loop?

Thanks!

    def read_bytes(self, num_bytes):

issue_title: T-measure acceleration
issue_body:
[Note: This isn't an immediate issue, but I want to jot down some thoughts here for future reference.]

The `hierarchy.tmeasure` implementation involves a [summation](https://github.com/craffel/mir_eval/blob/master/mir_eval/hierarchy.py#L146) that takes `O(n * w**2)` time, where `n` is the number of frames and `w` is the window length.  This calculation derives from counting the number of disordered triples `(q, i, j) || (q, j, i)` between estimate and reference.  We use a naive algorithm to do this via numpy ufuncs.

For small values of `w`, this is acceptably fast.  (There are other reasons to use small `w` for t-measures, as we describe in the paper, so this doesn't really come up in practice yet.)  However, when `w` is large -- or in the case of a new metric I'm developing for label hierarchies, `w==n` -- this approaches cubic time and is generally bad news.

This calculation can be reduced to `O(n w log(w))` with a divide-and-conquer algorithm for inversion counting.  For large `w`, this becomes `O(n^2 log(n))`, which is within a `log(n)` factor of the other frame-wise segmentation metrics (nce, pairwise-f, etc).

I'm implementing this enhancement in the fork that includes the label hierarchy measures.  Unless there are objections, I'll just include it with that PR, but we could also do it separately if there's sufficient need.

Environment: jedi==0.10.0, ptpython==0.36, ipython==5.2.2, pandas==0.19.0+479 (git).

Typing the following in the prompt:
```py
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> s = pd.Series()
>>> s.
```
Now, as soon as the last period is pressed, the following FutureWarning is printed at cursor location:
```py
/home/user/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/jedi/evaluate/compiled/__init__.py:328: FutureWarning: is_time_series is deprecated. Please use Series.index.is_all_dates
  getattr(obj, name)
```
Which makes the whole prompt input buffer hard to read:
```py

>>> import pandas as pd
>>> s = pd.Series()
>>> s./home/user/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/jedi/evaluate/compiled/__init__.py:328: FutureWarning: is_time_series is deprecated. Please use Series.index.is_all_dates
  getattr(obj, name)
|  ← cursor position
```
This repeats _every_ time `s` is followed by a `.`.

Downgrading to jedi 0.9.0 works.

issue_title: No autocompletion on stored objects
issue_body:
```
class Set:                                                                       
        def intersect(self, other):                                              
                return self                                                      
                                                                                 
class Map:                                                                       
        def domain(self):                                                        
                return Set()                                                     
                                                                                 
# Map().domain().<tab>                                                           
# The above line suggests 'intersect'. This is correct.                          
                                                                                 
# m = Map()                                                                      
# m.domain().<tab>                                                               
# The above line does not suggest any completions.    
```

It seems jedi cannot complete types, when starting from an in-memory object. Am I doing something wrong, is this a bug, or an inherently difficult problem to solve.

I would expect jedi to get the type from m._ _ class _ _ and then perform type completion as normal.

issue_title: Current version on PyPI doesn't play well with latest setuptools (PEP 440)
issue_body:
When trying to use a package that depends on jedi, I am getting the warning

```
PEP440Warning: 'jedi (0.8.1-final0)' is being parsed as a legacy, non PEP 440, version. You may find odd behavior and sort order. In particular it will be sorted as less than 0.0. It is recommend to migrate to PEP 440 compatible versions.
```

This is causing bad behavior for me, because jedi 0.8.1 is already installed, but it is trying to reinstall it, because it doesn't recognize the version 0.8.1-final0 as >= 0.8.1. 

Can you reupload 0.8.1 to PyPI without the -final0 in the version (and please don't use -final0 in future versions)?

issue_title: Release 0.11.0
issue_body:
There's a new release coming. The big feature is basically that parso is finally working. This means that jedi **from now on has a dependency**.

We also removed some side effects that were annoying for ipython users.

I'm trying to wrap this up in the next two weeks. Fixing a few bugs and trying to merge one or two pull requests.

~ Dave

issue_title: Support for python2 type comments
issue_body:
Currently, jedi only supports type hints for functions using python3 syntax.

It would be great to support type comments, which is widely used with python2.

```py
def foo(s):
    # type: (str) -> bool
    return s is not None and s.startswith('hello')
```

issue_title: GDFS thanks Fredrik Orre for his donation.
quit  question, how do you handle catchas?
Consider generating enums on multiple lines.
issue_body:
Presently I get:

```
enum ArrayName_e {ArrayName_ParameterName = 0, ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 1, ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 2, ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 3, ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 4, ArrayName_Count = 5};
```

I would personally prefer if enumerations were generated with one element per line such as:

```
enum ArrayName_e {
  ArrayName_ParameterName = 0,
  ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 1,
  ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 2,
  ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 3,
  ArrayName_NewArrayParameterElement = 4,
  ArrayName_Count = 5
};
```

If there is interest in this change I could presumably provide a PR.

issue_title: Inconsistent result for datetime.datetime.utcnow()
issue_body:
I'm getting inconsitent results when using freezegun on my local machine and our build server. On my local machine tests work, whereas on the build server tests fail.

```py
    @freeze_time('2024-04-19')
    def test_token_getter(app):
        """
        Test if this generates a token from the JWT payload.
    
        """
        payload = {
            'client_id': 'secret id',
            'scopes': [],
            'exp': 14293124283,
            'sub': 42
        }
        token = jwt.encode(payload, app.config['JWT_SECRET'])
        result = auth.tokengetter(token)
        assert result.client_id == 'secret id'
        assert result.scopes == []
>       assert result.expires == datetime.datetime(2422, 12, 6, 17, 18, 3)
E       assert FakeDatetime(2422, 12, 6, 18, 18, 3) == FakeDatetime(2422, 12, 6, 17, 18, 3)
E        +  where FakeDatetime(2422, 12, 6, 18, 18, 3) = <auth.Token object at 0x7fc8e2724320>.expires
E        +  and   FakeDatetime(2422, 12, 6, 17, 18, 3) = <class 'freezegun.api.FakeDatetime'>(2422, 12, 6, 17, 18, 3)
E        +    where <class 'freezegun.api.FakeDatetime'> = datetime.datetime
```

Both machines are in different time zones

```sh
me@build$ date +%z
+0300
```

```sh
me@laptop$ date +%z
+0200
```

From looking through the freezegun code it appears the default `tz_offset` is 0, which seems fine (no special handling for undefined values). My best explanation is that freezegun doesn't handle `utcnow()` properly.

issue_title: [RFE] Configurable list of excluded keys from the cooldown check
issue_body:
In the current state, the [cooldown](https://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py/blob/async/discord/ext/commands/cooldowns.py) functionality does not allow excluding certain keys (users/roles/etc.) from the cooldown check - e.g. when having a cooldown check with `BucketType.user` type[0], it would be nice to have a list of users for which this check would be always skipped (regular users vs admins).

The first solution which came to my mind would be creating a dummy class, which would inherit properties of the `Cooldown` class, but the `is_rate_limited()` method would always return zero. Then the `CooldownMapping.get_bucket()` would instantiate this class instead of the `Cooldown` one for all keys from the exclusion list.

But of course there are definitely better solutions to achieve this.

[0] `@commands.cooldown(rate=3, per=60, type=commands.BucketType.user)`

issue_title: Blocking behavior to get next message kills Apache Storm Workers?
issue_body:
In Storm UI I see the following:

```
java.lang.RuntimeException: subprocess heartbeat timeout at org.apache.storm.spout.ShellSpout$SpoutHeartbeatTimerTask.run(ShellSpout.java:311) at java.util.concurrent.Executors$RunnableAdapter.call(
```

I'm using petrel library (Python) to work with Storm.
https://github.com/AirSage/Petrel/

latest kafka-python (https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python) to work with kafka
Kafka 0.10
Storm 1.0.2
Python 3.5

java -version
java version "1.8.0_121"
Java(TM) SE Runtime Environment (build 1.8.0_121-b13)
Java HotSpot(TM) 64-Bit Server VM (build 25.121-b13, mixed mode)

**My kafka spout is pretty simple**

```python
import time

import config as cfg
from petrel import storm
from petrel.emitter import Spout

# enable loggers
log = cfg.config.loggers.kafka


class KafkaSpout(Spout):

    def __init__(self):
        self.stream = cfg.config.kafka
        # print(__file__)
        super().__init__(script=__file__)

    @classmethod
    def declareOutputFields(cls):
        return ['record']

    def nextTuple(self):
        try:
            record = next(self.stream)
            log.info("{}:{}:{}: key={} value={}".format(
                record.topic,
                record.partition,
                record.offset,
                record.key,
                record.value))
            storm.emit([record.value.decode()])
        finally:
            self.stream.commit()


def run():
    KafkaSpout().run()
```

So when messages are going into kafka - the whole process works properly. However, if messages delay then record = next(self.stream) blocks execution until new message comes in = and this crashes the worker 
see attached log: worker.log https://gist.github.com/dmitry-saritasa/188a70bef2dd402f2ad2648568ce2417

Any ideas how to overcome such problem?

issue_title: Offset fetch request does not support Kafka-committed offsets
issue_body:
The offset fetch request as implemented only supports offsets stored in Zookeeper. I've added support internally for fetching Kafka-committed offsets by adding the basic support needed for the consumer coordinator and adding a separate client method for fetching offsets from Kafka. The separate method is needed because fetching offsets from Kafka vs. from Zookeeper uses different versions of OffsetFetchRequest, even though the wire format is the same.

I've created PR #420 to add this functionality. We should be able to build the rest of the support for the consumer coordinator, including committing offsets to Kafka storage, from there.

issue_title: Tests you might find useful
issue_body:
Here are the tests for matrix stuffing from cvxpy. I'm removing them as part of switching to CVXcanon, but you might find them useful.

```
    def test_get_coefficients(self):
        """Test the get_coefficients function.
        """
        size = (5, 4)
        # Eye
        x = create_var(size)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(x)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(id_, x.data)
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat.todense(), sp.eye(20).todense())
        # Eye with scalar mult.
        x = create_var(size)
        A = create_const(5, (1, 1))
        coeffs = get_coefficients(mul_expr(A, x, size))
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat.todense(), 5*sp.eye(20).todense())
        # Promoted
        x = create_var((1, 1))
        coeffs = get_coefficients(promote(x, size))
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (20, 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, np.ones((20, 1)))
        # Normal
        size = (5, 5)
        x = create_var((5, 1))
        A = create_const(np.ones(size), size)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(mul_expr(A, x, (5, 1)))
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (5, 5))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat.todense(), A.data)
        # Blocks
        size = (5, 5)
        x = create_var(size)
        A = create_const(np.ones(size), size)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(mul_expr(A, x, size))
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (25, 25))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat.todense(),
         sp.block_diag(5*[np.ones(size)]).todense())
        # Scalar constant
        size = (1, 1)
        A = create_const(5, size)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(A)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(intf.size(mat), (1, 1))
        self.assertEqual(mat, 5)
        # Dense constant
        size = (5, 4)
        A = create_const(np.ones(size), size)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(A)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (size[0]*size[1], 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, np.ones(size))
        # Sparse constant
        size = (5, 5)
        A = create_const(sp.eye(5), size)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(A)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (size[0]*size[1], 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, sp.eye(5).todense())
        # Parameter
        size = (5, 4)
        param = Parameter(*size)
        param.value = np.ones(size)
        A = create_param(param, size)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(A)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (size[0]*size[1], 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, param.value)

    def test_transpose(self):
        """Test transpose op and coefficients.
        """
        size = (5, 4)
        x = create_var(size)
        expr = transpose(x)
        self.assertEqual(expr.size, (4, 5))
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        test_mat = np.mat(range(20)).T
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual((mat*test_mat).reshape((4, 5), order='F'),
            test_mat.reshape(size, order='F').T)

    def test_index(self):
        """Test the get_coefficients function for index.
        """
        size = (5, 4)
        # Eye
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,2,None))
        x = create_var(size)
        expr = index(x, (2, 2), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(id_, x.data)
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (4, 20))
        test_mat = np.mat(range(20)).T
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual((mat*test_mat).reshape((2, 2), order='F'),
            test_mat.reshape(size, order='F')[key])
        # Eye with scalar mult.
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,2,None))
        x = create_var(size)
        A = create_const(5, (1, 1))
        expr = mul_expr(A, x, size)
        expr = index(expr, (2, 2), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        test_mat = np.mat(range(20)).T
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual((mat*test_mat).reshape((2, 2), order='F'),
            5*test_mat.reshape(size, order='F')[key])
        # Promoted
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,2,None))
        x = create_var((1, 1))
        value = np.array(range(20)).reshape(size)
        A = create_const(value, size)
        prom_x = promote(x, (size[1], 1))
        expr = mul_expr(A, diag_vec(prom_x), size)
        expr = index(expr, (2, 2), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (4, 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, value[key])
        # Normal
        size = (5, 5)
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,1,None))
        x = create_var((5, 1))
        A = create_const(np.ones(size), size)
        expr = mul_expr(A, x, (5, 1))
        expr = index(expr, (2, 1), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (2, 5))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat.todense(), A.data[slice(0,2,None)])
        # Blocks
        size = (5, 5)
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,2,None))
        x = create_var(size)
        value = np.array(range(25)).reshape(size)
        A = create_const(value, size)
        expr = mul_expr(A, x, size)
        expr = index(expr, (2, 2), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (4, 25))
        test_mat = np.mat(range(25)).T
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual((mat*test_mat).reshape((2, 2), order='F'),
            (A.data*test_mat.reshape(size, order='F'))[key])
        # Scalar constant
        size = (1, 1)
        A = create_const(5, size)
        key = (slice(0,1,None), slice(0,1,None))
        expr = index(A, (1, 1), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(intf.size(mat), (1, 1))
        self.assertEqual(mat, 5)
        # Dense constant
        size = (5, 4)
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,1,None))
        value = np.array(range(20)).reshape(size)
        A = create_const(value, size)
        expr = index(A, (2, 1), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (2, 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, value[key])
        # Sparse constant
        size = (5, 5)
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,1,None))
        A = create_const(sp.eye(5), size)
        expr = index(A, (2, 1), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (2, 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, sp.eye(5).todense()[key])
        # Parameter
        size = (5, 4)
        key = (slice(0,2,None), slice(0,1,None))
        param = Parameter(*size)
        value = np.array(range(20)).reshape(size)
        param.value = value
        A = create_param(param, size)
        expr = index(A, (2, 1), key)
        coeffs = get_coefficients(expr)
        assert len(coeffs) == 1
        id_, mat = coeffs[0]
        self.assertEqual(mat.shape, (2, 1))
        self.assertItemsAlmostEqual(mat, param.value[key])
```

guide.duo.com is a domain hosted on google app engine, which is probably run by boringssl in the background.

The same host has been falsely detected by ssllabs and they fixed it in their dev version:

issue_title: List APIs/resource classes do not have access to uri template variables
issue_body:
Consider following example:


```python
class CategoriesList(PaginatedListAPI()):
    def list(params, meta, uri_template_variable, **kwargs):
        return [uri_template_variable] * 5

api.add_route("/v0/categories/{uri_template_variable}", ListResource())

issue_title: Bounding boxes become Triangles when Points Overlap
issue_body:
When creating a bounding box, move your pointer vertically or horizontally exactly in line with the original point. When you let go, the box is created and loos like a line segment, as expected. However, if you go to edit the box using its circular handles, the bounding box has lost a point, becoming a bounding triangle! It retains some of the movement behaviors of rectangles, but mostly just glitches around the screen as you drag its handles. issue_title: Filter Plugin - Not specific enough find
issue_body:
Mottie! I have one little tweak/fix that was making something conflict for me.

In the filter plugin, around line 122 there is this:

```javascript
v = tbl.find('.filter').map(function(){ return ($(this).val() || '').toLowerCase(); }).get();
```

That was conflicting with an icon that had class "filter" for me in my header, causing all the filters to be off by 1.
A more specific find helped with the issue:

```javascript
v = tbl.find('thead tr.filters .filter').map(function(){ return ($(this).val() || '').toLowerCase(); }).get();
```

Thanks for the great work,
-Seth

issue_title: Java exception when querying calculated field with divide by zero
issue_body:
Thanks for the library, it's a definite sanity restorer.

I've been using the library to query a FileMaker Pro database with JDBC, and it seems to work well. However, there is a calculated column in one of the tables of the form X/Y. If both X,Y are zero a None is returned which is fine, but if X is non-zero it appears an exception is raised from the Java side and propagates up. If you try/catch through this, it appears `fetchone()` gets a value from the last row.

The exception is 
`com.filemaker.jdbc.FMSQLException:` [FileMaker][FileMaker JDBC] For input string: "?": ?`

Is there a way to specify that the column should produce a None, or is that part buried inside the Java library?

issue_title: Azure DataWarehouse
issue_body:
ODBC - 13.0.4 ( the latest MS one ) 
pyodbc - 4.0.16.0
python2.7
64-bit everything

I am running pyodbc on Linux, mostly ubuntu, the odbc version is the latest from MS. In general, it works really well, but I am having an issue that I am having a difficult time reproducing. 

In all of the code we are running, there are many thousands of queries running against a single database, after a few days, we see queries slow down significantly eventually causing me to have to pause/resume which seems to clear it up.
Though it may be something I can solve on the database side, I've solved similar problems with the same database ( different codebase ) by adding explicit closes when the code completes or raises an exception. 

I went through the code, but I am weak on C++, is there any situation where a dead connection could be left executing? 
I am interested in anyone's input on this, I know it is likely on the database side, but on the off-chance there is a connection leaking somewhere, I'm opening this issue.

issue_title: Question: How is the Exception handling supposed to work?
issue_body:
**Background**
I have a program in which I run multiple Listeners for a long period of time, usually somewhere over half an hour to even over an hour at a time. It is very useful to me if exceptions anywhere in the program are reported immediately, as for most parts of the code unittests is not possible (given the structure and nature of the program), so I can debug issues as soon as I see them appear.

Currently, the pynput Listeners hide the exceptions from me, completely. I lost quite a bit of time tracking down the issue to the pynput Listeners. I was a bit disappointed when I discovered that I can only catch the errors upon joining a Thread, which is only done after I have performed a testing session.

**Observed Behaviour**
Using my fork of the repository, I have determined the behaviour to be the following:
`lib/pynput/_utils/__init__.py`
```python
@classmethod
def _emitter(cls, f):
    @functools.wraps(f)
    def inner(self, *args, **kwargs):
        try:
            return f(self, *args, **kwargs)  # Call the callback for the event
        except Exception as e:  # Exception occurred in callback
            if not isinstance(e, self._HANDLED_EXCEPTIONS):
                # Schedule for raising upon .join()
                self._queue.put(
                    None if isinstance(e, cls.StopException)
                    else sys.exc_info())
                self.stop()  # Cleanup
            # XXX Never reached
            raise
    return inner
```

**Expected Behaviour**
I am not familiar with Threading daemons, but I would expect the error to be re-raised at the end of the `try-except`-block. However, the `raise` point is never reached, thus I do not see anything appear on `stderr`. Is this the desired behaviour? Because of it is, I would like propose a PR for my branch of the repository.

**Other Details**
Operating System: Ubuntu 18.04 64-bit
Back-end: Xorg

issue_title: coverxygen produces negative execution counts for undocumented functions
issue_body:
Thanks a lot for this very useful tool!

I noticed that the `output_print_lcov` method will produce negative execution counts in the `lcov` output format when it encounters an undocumented object:

https://github.com/psycofdj/coverxygen/blob/ce7e1534b4c7b2bc91d8997483c87ddd2718f5a2/coverxygen/__init__.py#L226

This results in output like the following:
```
...
DA:53,1
DA:49,1
DA:61,-1
DA:62,-1
DA:63,-1
DA:64,-1
DA:60,1
...
```

lcov (at least in version 1.13) will process the file properly, but complains:

```
lcov: WARNING: negative counts found in tracefile
```

The service `codecov.io` on the other hand processes the output, but counts negative counts as covered, leading to an incorrect 100% coverage.

Replacing all counts of `-1` in the output with `0` (e.g. with `sed -i -e 's/,-1$/,0/g' <outputfile>`) causes the warning to disappear from the `lcov` and `genhtml` output while still producing the correct coverage report.

More importantly (at least for me), submitting the file with the counts set to `0` instead of `-1` to `codecov.io` results in the results being parsed correctly and the coverage information being available through `codecov.io`.

issue_title: plumbum.cli.Flag shouldn't print the default on execution of --help
issue_body:
```
./a.py --help
--no-ut                     Set this flag to skip the unit-tests; *the default is True*
./b.py --help
--release                      Deploy in release mode (no QA baggage); *the default is True*
```

This is *very* confusing...
It's pointless to print the default value for flags which are just switching a state, and the default value is for the in-code variable, which for the above are:

```python
    ut = cli.Flag("--no-ut", default=True, help="Set this flag to skip the unit-tests")
    dev = cli.Flag("--release", default=True, help="Deploy in release mode (no QA baggage)")
```

a fix can be:
```python
--- a/plumbum/cli/switches.py
+++ b/plumbum/cli/switches.py
@@ -179,7 +179,7 @@ class SwitchAttr(object):
 
     def __init__(self, names, argtype = str, default = None, list = False, argname = "VALUE", **kwargs):
         self.__doc__ = "Sets an attribute"  # to prevent the help message from showing SwitchAttr's docstring
-        if "help" in kwargs and default:
+        if "help" in kwargs and default and argtype is not None:
             kwargs["help"] += "; the default is %r" % (default,)
 
         switch(names, argtype = argtype, argname = argname, list = list, **kwargs)(self)
```

issue_title: Scaling issue when inserting jpg image, which was converted from png
issue_body:
python3.4, OSX Yosemite, xlsxwriter 0.7.2

Issue:
When inserting a .jpg image into a worksheet, the resulting image in Excel 2011 is scaled incorrectly.   I have not tried another version of Excel.  Instead of 100%, it is scaled to height:  6720 % and width: 7950 %.  If I manually change the image size in Excel back to 100%, then the image is correctly shown.

Note:
It would seem that this is not an Excel 2011 issue, because the same image can be inserted into Excel using a ruby gem "axlsx" (see below).  Be that as it may, I am limited to python for this project.

Question:  If this is not fixable, can you suggest a workaround?  Thanks in advance for any insight.

This is the code:
```python
wb = Workbook()
ws = wb.add_worksheet()
ws.insert_image(0,0,'test.jpg')
wb.filename = 'test.xlsx'
wb.close()
```
I'm attaching 2 versions of an image.  Both images have pixel width x height of:  1199 x 790
* The original image (test.png).  The issue does not occur with this image file.
* The .jpg image (test.jpg) which was converted to .jpg using ImageMagick.  The issue occurs only with the .jpg version of the file.

Additional Note:  The issue does not occur when using the ruby gem 'axlsx' to do the equivalent function with the same image files in the same environment.  The ruby code looks like this:

```ruby
p = Axlsx::Package.new
wb = p.workbook
ws = wb.add_worksheet
ws.add_image({image_src: 'test.jpg', noSelect: false, noMove: true}) do |image|
   image.start_at(0,0)
   image.width = 1199
   image.height = 790
end
p.serialize('test-ruby.xlsx')
```

import xlsxwriter

url,url_str = url.split('#',1)
```

issue_title: module 'django.forms.widgets' has no attribute 'ChoiceFieldRenderer'
Unused Service-Client Connection
issue_body:
If a service_client only subscribes/xsubscribes to a service, even then it creates a long-lasting connection to it, which is never used since the publishing service either uses Redis or creates a new connection to send packets. 

This unused connection can potentially be used for publish/xpublish and get rid of Vyked's dependency on Redis and save repeated 'get_subscribers' calls to the registry.

issue_title: RpcError when lease has expired is raised unhandled.
issue_body:
Hi,

While testing how a lease works i noticed that upon a lease expiration this unhandled exception is raised:

**grpc._channel._Rendezvous: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated with (StatusCode.NOT_FOUND, etcdserver: requested lease not found)>**

I used the python debugger and the error handling seems to fail in the _handle_errors decorator [here](https://github.com/kragniz/python-etcd3/blob/master/etcd3/client.py#L34)

pdb output:

> except grpc.RpcError as exc:
(Pdb) n
> /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/etcd3/client.py(33)handler()
-> code = exc.code()
(Pdb) n
> /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/etcd3/client.py(34)handler()
-> exception = _EXCEPTIONS_BY_CODE.get(code)
(Pdb) print code
StatusCode.NOT_FOUND
(Pdb) print _EXCEPTIONS_BY_CODE.get(code)
None
(Pdb) n
> /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/etcd3/client.py(35)handler()
-> if exception is None:
(Pdb) n
> /usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/etcd3/client.py(36)handler()
-> raise

The code i used to test it:

```python
import etcd3
from etcd3 import exceptions
import time

etcd_con = etcd3.client()

lease = etcd_con.lease(ttl=1)

time.sleep(3)

try:
    etcd_con.put('/my_key', '1', lease.id)
except exceptions.Etcd3Exception:
    pass
```

It is also strange that if the process sleeps for 2 seconds instead of 3 the lease does not expire as expected since the ttl is set to 1 sec.

Best Regards

issue_title: Slugifying Chinese characters with multiple readings
issue_body: This is probably beyond doable, since it would require extensive lookups, but slugifying can result in erroneous output if a given character has multiple readings (多音字), and context would be required to determine the correct slug, e.g. 行 is correctly rendered 'xing' when it stands alone, but 'hang' it would have to be slugified as 'hang' when it stands with 银行 (銀行) in a compound word. (See [3rd paragraph in this](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_characters#Function).)

issue_title: Assignment Error with get_order_book?
issue_body:
### What are you trying to achieve?

Call the get_order_book function.


``` python
# code sample

api = krakenex.API(APIPublic, APIPrivate)
k = KrakenAPI(api)
k.get_order_book('XETHZEUR','10','True')


The relevant function is:


    def get_order_book(self, pair, count=100, ascending=False):
        """Get order book (market depth).

        Return a ``pd.DataFrame`` for both asks and bids for a given pair.

        Parameters
        ----------
        pair : str
            Asset pair to get market depth for.

        count : int, optional (default=100)
            Maximum number of asks/bids. Per default, get the latest 100
            bids and asks.

        ascending : bool, optional (default=False)
            If set to True, the data frame will be sorted with the most recent
            date in the last position. When set to False, the most recent date
            is in the first position.

        Returns
        -------
        asks : pd.DataFrame
            The ask side table.
            index = datetime
            price
            volume
            time (unixtime)

        bids : pd.DataFrame
            The bid side table.
            index = datetime
            price
            volume
            time (unixtime)

        Raises
        ------
        HTTPError
            An HTTP error occurred.

        KrakenAPIError
            A kraken.com API error occurred.

        CallRateLimitError
            The call rate limiter blocked the query.

        """

        # create data dictionary
        data = {arg: value for arg, value in locals().items() if
                arg != 'self' and value is not None}

        # query
        res = self.api.query_public('Depth', data=data)

        # check for error
        if len(res['error']) > 0:
            raise KrakenAPIError(res['error'])

        # create dataframe
        asks = pd.DataFrame(res['result'][pair]['asks'])
        bids = pd.DataFrame(res['result'][pair]['bids'])

        # column names
        cols = ['price', 'volume', 'time']

        if not asks.empty:
            asks.columns = cols
            asks['dtime'] = pd.to_datetime(asks.time, unit='s')
            asks.sort_values('dtime', ascending=ascending, inplace=True)
            asks.set_index('dtime', inplace=True)

        if not bids.emtpy:
            bids.columns = cols
            bids['dtime'] = pd.to_datetime(bids.time, unit='s')
            bids.sort_values('dtime', ascending=ascending, inplace=True)
            bids.set_index('dtime', inplace=True)

        return asks, bids

```
### What do you expect to happen?

I expect 2 DataFrames to returned for Asks and Bids.

At least some other functions from the same library DO work.


### What happens instead?

An error results. From my understanding, the API exchange happens successfully, because giving an invalid trading pair name gives an API error from their server. The 'asks' dataframe is filled successfully, but there is then a problem with the 'bids' dataframe as the line

  if not bids.emtpy:

causes an error.


``` python
# error message

if not bids.emtpy:
return object.__getattribute__(self, name)
AttributeError: 'DataFrame' object has no attribute 'emtpy'

```
Sorry if this is a stupid error on my part.

issue_title: Let user be explicit about origins encoding: plain, glob, regex or regex_partial
issue_body:
See https://github.com/corydolphin/flask-cors/pull/160#issuecomment-226674064 which discussed a security issue when regular strings were considered regexes.

Even if glob encoding isn't supported, it isn't neither prohibited explicitly, so when user uses Glob-style
```
origins = [ "https://api.*.company.com" ]
```
he should be aware that Origin `https://api-company-com.malware.com` will match by setting origin matching schema to `regex_partial`

What would be great to have:
* plain - no regex matching is done, all origins should be explicit
* glob - wildcards are expanded
* regex - origin is matched to a regex, but with `^...$` wrappers, so no domain hijacking should be possible
* regex_partial - origin is matched to a regex, but user has full freedom for matching

issue_title: Changing Field Order In Subclass
issue_body:
Really enjoying the `dataclass` decorator and can't wait for it to be standard in Python 3.7. I've been playing around with it in 3.6 and came across a use-case that I can't seem to make work intuitively. Basically, `Point2D` is a point with a units field, and a default value to simplify things. `Blob` is a subclass of `Point2D` which is simply a point with a diameter. It needs to be a subclass so that it can inherit the helper methods on `Point2D` and so type-checking allows it to be used anywhere `Point2D` can be.

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class Unit(Enum):
    Millimeters = 'mm'
    Inches = 'in'


@dataclass
class Point2D:
    x: float
    y: float
    units: Unit = Unit.Inches

    def distance_to(self, point: 'Point2D') -> float:
        """ Calculate distance to another point """


@dataclass
class Blob(Point2D):
    diameter: float
```

This seems to run into two main issues. First, `Blob` can't add a required `diameter` field as it would cause a non-default argument to follow a default argument in the generated `__init__`. This could be worked around with a custom `__init__`, but that would still leave us with the second problem: the order of the fields in `repr` and `astuple`. Ideally we'd want those to be: `x`, `y`, `diameter`, `units` as that makes more sense than `x`, `y`, `units`, `diameter`.

What if class inheritance where the child class defines a superset of the parent's fields was a special case which re-defined the order of the fields? Would turn the above `Blob` definition into:

```python
@dataclass
class Blob(Point2D):
    x: float
    y: float
    diameter: float
    units: Unit = Unit.Inches
```

I suppose this is a bit 'dangerous' as it's magic and someone could inadvertently redefine the order of the fields while subclassing when they simply intended to give all fields a default, but listed them in a slightly different order. As such, perhaps it could be enabled by a new keyword arg to `dataclass`, such as `allow_reorder`.

Implementation could be pretty straightforward, before [line 802](https://github.com/ericvsmith/dataclasses/blob/master/dataclasses.py#L802) add something like:

```python
if set([f.name for f in cls_fields]).issuperset(set(fields.keys())):
    fields.clear()
```

Actually, while typing this all up I realized that the implementation was simple enough that I just went ahead and did it in my own fork, 183ccfea97feab12a657bd1971db816a7ae0998d. Only played with it a little but so far so good, it's not a very complicated change.

issue_title: Error on update_cells when first row of values is empty
issue_body:
I'm getting an `integer division or modulo by zero` error when I try to use the method `update_cells` and my first row is empty. The lib needs this row as reference to calculate the batch request.

### Explanation:
```py
# Data to save
values = [
   [],
   ['Name', 'Cod', 'Desc'],
   ['Jhon', '1', 'Lorem ipsum']
]

# Trying to save the data on the worksheet
worksheet.update_cells(crange='A1', values=base_values, extend=True)
```
#### **Exception: integer division or modulo by zero**

------------ 

### The error stack on `/client.py`:
```py
def sh_update_range(self, spreadsheet_id, body, batch, parse=True):
    cformat = 'USER_ENTERED' if parse else 'RAW'
    batch_limit = GOOGLE_SHEET_CELL_UPDATES_LIMIT
    if body['majorDimension'] == 'ROWS':

        # === Error is here, when try to calc the batch_length ===
        batch_length = int(batch_limit / len(body['values'][0]))
        # ========================================================

        num_rows = len(body['values'])
    else:
        batch_length = int(batch_limit / len(body['values']))
        num_rows = len(body['values'][0])
```

### Possible solution
In my opinion is better calculate this `batch_length` counting each element from every row/col. So we can be a more accurate cell counting to check if a request in batch is needed.

issue_title: YAML syntax in Fenced code block?
issue_body:
As mentioned in #12 about an enhanced version of csv2table in [pandoc-table-csv-test/panflute-csv2table.ipynb at master · ickc/pandoc-table-csv-test](https://github.com/ickc/pandoc-table-csv-test/blob/master/ipynb/panflute-csv2table.ipynb), I have some questions on the YAML syntax required in the fenced code block.

First, it doesn't allow an opening `---` row, e.g.

```markdown
~~~csv
---
table-width: 1.2
...
**_Fruit_**,~~Price~~,_Number_,`Advantages`
*Bananas~1~*,$1.34,12~units~,"Benefits of eating bananas 
(**Note the appropriately
rendered block markdown**):    

- _built-in wrapper_        
- ~~**bright color**~~

"
*Oranges~2~*,$2.10,5^10^~units~,"Benefits of eating oranges:

- **cures** scurvy
- `tasty`"
~~~
```

will treat the `table-width: 1.2` after the first `---` as the actual table (i.e. not YAML), not the content below.

Second, if there's no YAML like this:

```
~~~csv
**_Fruit_**,~~Price~~,_Number_,`Advantages`
*Bananas~1~*,$1.34,12~units~,"Benefits of eating bananas 
(**Note the appropriately
rendered block markdown**):    

- _built-in wrapper_        
- ~~**bright color**~~

"
*Oranges~2~*,$2.10,5^10^~units~,"Benefits of eating oranges:

- **cures** scurvy
- `tasty`"
~~~
```

It will have an error:

```bash
yaml.scanner.ScannerError: while scanning an alias
  in "<unicode string>", line 1, column 1:
    **_Fruit_**,~~Price~~,_Number_,` ... 
```

i.e. empty YAML still requires a `---`/`...` row to start with.

issue_title: Improvement: Faster exit on shutdown for thread pooling
issue_body:
```
I propose changing
        while True:
            try:
                work_item = work_queue.get(block=True, timeout=0.1)
            except queue.Empty:
                executor = executor_reference()
                # Exit if:
                #   - The interpreter is shutting down OR
                #   - The executor that owns the worker has been collected OR
                #   - The executor that owns the worker has been shutdown.
                if _shutdown or executor is None or executor._shutdown:
                    return
                del executor
            else:
                work_item.run()

To
        while True:
            executor = executor_reference()
            # Exit if:
            #   - The interpreter is shutting down OR
            #   - The executor that owns the worker has been collected OR
            #   - The executor that owns the worker has been shutdown.
            if _shutdown or executor is None or executor._shutdown:
                return
            del executor
            try:
                work_item = work_queue.get(block=True, timeout=0.1)
            except queue.Empty:
                pass
            else:
                work_item.run()

This would allow you not to have to wait for workers to go through the pool. 
Since we just wait in shutdown for the threads to finish, it should (as far as 
I can see) be safe to just make the threads unconditionally check whether they 
should exit and do so. As it is, you *have to* wait for all submitted work to 
finish before exiting interpreter which is imo inconvenient.
```

Original issue reported on code.google.com by `seppo.yl...@gmail.com` on 10 Jun 2013 at 9:24

issue_title: Windows install issue
issue_body:
Heya Kyle,

Love the look of your themes but am having trouble installing them on Windows using Anaconda (2.4.0 64 bit). I'm running this in cmd.exe but I've also tried it using Git Bash (2.7.0 64 bit) with the same result.

    >pip install git+https://github.com/dunovank/jupyter-themes.git
    Collecting git+https://github.com/dunovank/jupyter-themes.git
      Cloning https://github.com/dunovank/jupyter-themes.git to c:\users\matt~1.tre\appdata\local\temp\pip-hmy4xr-build
    Collecting sh (from jupyter-themes==0.1)
      Using cached sh-1.11.tar.gz
        Complete output from command python setup.py egg_info:
        Traceback (most recent call last):
          File "<string>", line 20, in <module>
          File "c:\users\matt~1.tre\appdata\local\temp\pip-build-v_m6bu\sh\setup.py", line 4, in <module>
            import sh
          File "sh.py", line 37, in <module>
            support." % __version__)
        ImportError: sh 1.11 is currently only supported on linux and osx. please install pbs 0.110 (http://pypi.python.org/pypi/pbs) for windows support.

        ----------------------------------------
    Command "python setup.py egg_info" failed with error code 1 in c:\users\matt~1.tre\appdata\local\temp\pip-build-v_m6bu\sh

But pbs is already installed and appears to be the correct version:

    >pip install pbs
    Requirement already satisfied (use --upgrade to upgrade): pbs in c:\anaconda\lib\site-packages

    >pip show pbs
    ---
    Metadata-Version: 2.0
    Name: pbs
    Version: 0.110

I've only take a quick look but here's what seems to be happening...hoping you can shed more light on this!

So, although the [documentation](http://amoffat.github.io/sh/) doesn't mention platform compatibility, the internet seems to suggest ([1](http://www.makeuseof.com/tag/move-shell-scripts-sh-py-awesome/), [2](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/28618906/porting-sh-1-11-based-code-to-windows), [3](http://www.blog.pythonlibrary.org/2014/08/07/the-new-sh-package-a-subprocess-wrapper/)) that sh.py doesn't support Windows. It seems that the installer is aborting as soon as it detects that we're trying to install a non-Windows supported module on Windows. 

I think using pbs as an alternative is a red herring; the project is no longer supported. And you'd have to change your code to use pbs anyway.

However, it seems that the only use of sh here is for cp (in `__init__.py`). You're already using shutil - which *is* cross-platform - and also contains a copy function. Would you be able to change to using [shutil.copy](https://docs.python.org/2/library/shutil.html#shutil.copy) instead of sh.cp?

issue_body:
hi guys, I'm referring to the codes in social/apps/django_app/context_processors.py/backends:

```
return {'backends': LazyDict(lambda: user_backends_data(request.user, BACKENDS, Storage))}
```

The context processor hits DB every time it's called, while it's possible that context processors are called many times in one view (in my project, a pagination package does it). As a result, this SQL was executed many times:

```
SELECT `social_auth_usersocialauth`.`id`, ...... FROM `social_auth_usersocialauth` WHERE `social_auth_usersocialauth`.`user_id` = 1
```

It's a significant waste. I think it's worth to do a cache. Such as:

```
psa_backends = getattr(request, 'psa_backends', None)
if psa_backends is None:
    psa_backends = LazyDict(lambda: user_backends_data(request.user,BACKENDS,Storage))
    request.psa_backends = psa_backends
return {'backends': psa_backends}
```

issue_title: passing an existing session (with proxies that require authentication) to cloudflare-scrape
issue_body:
I am having an issue using an existing session with cloudflare-scrape. 

Code 1 works and goes through the proxy authentication just fine.

_Code 1_
```
s = requests.Session()
s.proxies.update({'http': 'http://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port', 'https': 'https://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port'})
s.trust_env=False;  #this line must be there otherwise auth wont work
res = s.get('http://python.org')
```

I tried passing that session when creating a scrapper as seen in Code 2.
_Code 2_
```
s=requests.Session()
s.proxies.update({'http': 'http://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port', 'https': 'https://username:password@proxy_ip_address:port'})
s.auth = HTTPProxyAuth('username', 'password') 
s.trust_env=False
scraper = cfscrape.create_scraper(sess=s) 
scraper.get('http://python.org').content
```

However, I got the following error, 
```
requests.exceptions.ProxyError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='python.org', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: / (Caused by ProxyError('Cannot connect to prox
y.', error('Tunnel connection failed: 407 Proxy Authentication Required',))).
```

Please note that I would get the same error in Code 1 if I commented out the s.trust_env=False line, which lead me to believe that the _trust_env_ variable is not populated when create_scraper is called. 
My conclusion was correct as you can see blow _trust_env_ was not one of the attributes set by create_scraper.
_original code from create_scraper_

```

I tried to simply add _trust_env_ to the attrs so that create_srcaper would set it but It did not help.
```
 if sess:
        attrs = ["trust_env","auth", "cert", "cookies", "headers", "hooks", "params", "proxies", "data"]
        for attr in attrs:

issue_title: Improve usage documentation with examples
issue_body:
Hello @byt3bl33d3r,

I thought it could be handy to document accepted formats and/or example for most of available options.

For instance, it is not obvious which is the correct format for the PtH `-H` option: for CrackMapExec it is `lmhash:nthash` (it may varies for other common tools). The colon is important and shouldn't be forgotten even if the user only has a lmhash or nthash. 
In that case I propose to go:
* from this
"`  -H HASH               NTLM hash(es) or file containing NTLM hashes`"
* to that
"`  -H HASH               NTLM hash(es) or file containing NTLM hashes (-H <lmhash>:<nthash>)`"

Same approach for common option such as `-k`.
Cheers

issue_title: Add support for non-string type keys for dicts
issue_body:
At present (version 0.1.9), only ``dict`` with keys that are entirely unicode strings (``str`` in Python 3.x and ``unicode`` in Python 2.x) can be written to an HDF5 file.

Issue #24 is with regards to supporting the other three string types as keys. Support is also needed for when those other string types can't be converted to UTF-8 or are not a string type.

A straight forward way to add this support is for the keys and values to be packed into separate numpy object arrays and written as if the ``dict`` was ``{'keys': keys, 'values': values}``. A new HDF5 Attribute will be needed for ``dict`` types to indicate whether it is stored as separate keys and values or whether it is stored the current way (Dataset and Group names are keys and their contents the values).

issue_body:
When infer_missing is set to True, it by-pass default argument.  
Consider the following code :

```
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from dataclasses_json import DataClassJsonMixin

@dataclass()
class MyObject(DataClassJsonMixin):
    id: str
    name: str

```

The `category` is replaced by `null`, not by the default value `my_object`

issue_title: Default template code must be altered as some HTML tags are escaped
issue_body:
In a lot of the calendar templates, the following template tag is invoked to display arrows for the next/previous calendar view:

`{% prevnext "tri_month_calendar" calendar periods.month "F Y"%}`

However, although this works on a local server, bring this online will result in the HTML tags of the function `prevnext` to be escaped (at least for Heroku, which I used). Thus, the HTML code will appear as raw HTML on the webpage.

To fix this, the `autoescape` and `endautoescape`, can be added to avoid escaping the HTML tags, like so:
`{% autoescape off %}{% prevnext "tri_month_calendar" calendar periods.month "F Y"%}{% endautoescape %}`

issue_title: Flask-Login for multiple users table(like admin and user)
Session protection triggering unnecessarily, causing "remember cookie" to be wrongfully cleared when in "strong" mode
issue_body:
I've been working through Miguel Grinberg's "Flask Web Development" book, which makes use of Flask-Login. I'm using version v0.3.2. The book's example project sets the session protection to "strong" mode and utilizes the "remember me" feature. I noticed that my login session is only remembered on the very first request upon returning to the site after closing my browser then re-opening it. On the second request, my login session is wiped out. I dug through the code to help me see what was going on. Here's what I came up with:

1. User opens browser, navigates to site
2. User logs in, with "remember me" enabled
3. session['_id'] is initialized with call to _create_identifier()
4. The "remember cookie" is created
5. User closes browser, while still logged in
6. User opens browser, navigates to site, expecting their session to still be active
7. User info is loaded from the "remember cookie" on the first request (gives the user the impression they're still logged in and everything is fine)
8. On the very next request, however, session protection is wrongfully triggered due to there no longer being a session['_id'] value (therefore it doesn't match the per-request-generated challenge value)
9. Since session protection is triggered while the mode is "strong", all of the SESSION_KEYS are popped from the session and the "remember cookie" is cleared
10. The user is now logged out

The issue is not quite as bad in "basic" mode. Session protection is still wrongfully triggered, on all requests after the first one, due to the missing session['_id'] value, but the only side effect is that session['_fresh'] is set to False (which is the value it already would have anyway as a result of a previous call to _load_from_cookie()). The user still remains logged in after closing and re-opening the browser in "basic" mode.

I experimented with the code and came up with an idea that seemed to accomplish my goal. I altered _set_cookie() and _load_from_cookie() in such a way that the "remember cookie" stores session['_id'] along with session['user_id']. With this method in place, session protection is not wrongfully triggered (see step 8 above), since session['_id'] was loaded in from the "remember cookie." I'm not sure if this is the best long-term solution or if it's a potential security issue. It seems that if the cookie ended up in the wrong hands, session protection would kick in and drop the session when it compares the _id value from the cookie to the newly generated one which uses the attacker's IP and user agent string.

issue_title: `observation_period_end` should default to the max transaction time
issue_body:
In `utils.calibration_and_holdout_data(transactions, customer_id_col, datetime_col, calibration_period_end, observation_period_end=datetime.today(), freq='D', datetime_format=None)`: `datetime.today()` is set once when the file is first read. This can be confusing when dealing with historical data, as the holdout column and evaluation results become dependent on the time the process is run.

It'd be better to default to `None` and then in the function body do:
```python
if observation_period_end is None:
    observation_period_end = transactions[datetime_col].max()
```

issue_title: CORS headers not being added to the response despite endpoint being decorated
Async update TextBox background color (and other options)?
issue_body:
First off, thanks so much for this project! I made my first real gui and and my chemistry (read: non-coder) business partner is stoked to not be forced to the command line to use the tools i make for him.

This issue is about adding more `kwargs` to the `.Update()` method of a text box. My application needs to be almost entirely asychronous as i am reading values from a serial port and I would love to be able to display some information to the user by say changing background colors based on these events. (see screenshot below) I assume this should be just a matter of passing a few more args down to the TKInter objects and re-instantiating them? 

Probably deserves another issue but...: I'd really love to have all the elements be async-able . Right now I update a `ListBox` element after reading in a file by tearing down the window and regenerating a new form but this is really hacky and slow.

issue_title: Action functions can't handle type hints
issue_body:
Trying to use type hints for action function parameters returns this error:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 2309, in __call__
    return self.wsgi_app(environ, start_response)
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 2295, in wsgi_app
    response = self.handle_exception(e)
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1741, in handle_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/_compat.py", line 35, in reraise
    raise value
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 2292, in wsgi_app
    response = self.full_dispatch_request()
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1815, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.handle_user_exception(e)
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1718, in handle_user_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/_compat.py", line 35, in reraise
    raise value
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1813, in full_dispatch_request
    rv = self.dispatch_request()
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask/app.py", line 1799, in dispatch_request
    return self.view_functions[rule.endpoint](**req.view_args)
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask_assistant/core.py", line 261, in _flask_assitant_view_func
    result = self._map_intent_to_view_func(view_func)()
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask_assistant/core.py", line 361, in _map_intent_to_view_func
    arg_names = self._func_args(view_func)
  File "<project_dir>/venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/flask_assistant/core.py", line 357, in _func_args
    argspec = inspect.getargspec(f)
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python/3.6.4_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.6/lib/python3.6/inspect.py", line 1075, in getargspec
    raise ValueError("Function has keyword-only parameters or annotations"
ValueError: Function has keyword-only parameters or annotations, use getfullargspec() API which can support them
```

The reason for this is that [`inspect.getargspec`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/inspect.html#classes-and-functions) can't handle type annotations and is deprecated in favor of `inspect.getfullargspec`. According to the documentation the latter should be a drop-in replacement, but this should probably be tested.

issue_title: bintrees is deprecated, suggests sortedcontainers
issue_body:
Hi, I'm the author of `sortedcontainers` and noticed that you're using the `bintrees` module. If you look at the PyPI page for [bintrees](https://pypi.org/project/bintrees/) then you'll see that development has stopped and `sortedcontainers` is recommended instead.

I'd love to see `sortedcontainers` used in more places. If I created a pull request which replaced `bintrees` with `sortedcontainers`, would that be merged?

issue_title: Define pretty printers for more standard library modules
issue_body:
Here's a to-do list of standard library modules to write `prettyprinter` definitions for. This is just a list of the modules that I would personally find useful to have definitions for, so feel free to add to the list.

If it's not checked, it's not being worked on, so feel free to submit a PR. You can submit partial definitions for a module.

- [ ] Built-in Exceptions
- [ ] `collections`
- [ ] `re`
- [ ] `functools` (`partial`)
- [ ] `pathlib`
- [ ] `logging`
- [ ] `email`
- [ ] `inspect` (`Signature`, etc.)
- [ ] `ast`
- [ ] `unittest.mock`
- [ ] `urllib.parse` (`ParseResult`, `SplitResult`)
- [ ] `ipaddress`
- [ ] `argparse`
- [ ] `io`
- [ ] `hashlib`

As the number of definitions grows, it may become infeasible to import all these modules along with `prettyprinter` to register their definitions. A mechanism for deferred type definition, such as [the one used by `IPython.lib.pretty`](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/IPython/lib/pretty.py#L407-L423) would be useful.

issue_title: Thread / eventlet safety of context managers?
issue_body:
I was looking at the inner workings of the translation fallback mechanisms, mostly looking at the helper functions defined at /utils.py . I was curious how you handled concurrency for the context manager `fallbacks()`. This manager function directly modifies settings.ENABLE_FALLBACKS without accounting for any concurrency?

This won't cause any problems for sync workers, but both threaded and async workers can (and most likely will) run into problems. I highly recommend the use of of a Thread Local's object. If you look at Django's source code you will find plently of examples e.g. https://github.com/django/django/blob/5a6f70b4281817656db2f36c5919036d38fcce7f/django/core/cache/__init__.py

issue_title: API endpoints not being auto-detected by django-rest-swagger
issue_body:
Not sure how much of a bug this is, but I thought it was worth mentioning as there may be other implications to this issue.

I noticed that django-rest-swagger wasn't finding most of the API endpoints added by django-rest-framework-social-oauth2. 

I believe the below change would fix this. I have not tested fully, but if this fix is considered relevant, let me know and I'll confirm that it works and will open a PR for it.

views.py line:21 ConvertTokenView
Currently extending `django.views.generic.View`. As a result, this isn't a django-rest-framework view and thus doesn't have the attributes that other tools similar to django-rest-swagger look for. Changing this to extend from `rest_framework.views.APIView` should fix this. 

I extended `ConvertTokenView` in my code to make this change. It did fix it, but I ran into an issue with permissions though. That requires adding `permission_classes = (AllowAny,)` as this is an endpoint that should have security on it. 

This fix would need to be made for every endpoint, with the exception of `invalidate_sessions`.

Again, let me know if this is something you want in the project and I'll go down the path of PR'ing.
