issue_title: enforceHTTPS causes unwanted redirect.
issue_body:
We have a number of APIs that service an AngularJS application, one of these APIs is legacy and will be replaced in time but for now we proxy requests from the newer API to the legacy one (HapiJS to nginx/PHP/FastCGI). This works perfectly on the Deis platform in both SSL and non-SSL.

However, when we turn on `enforceHTTPS` to make sure that all requests are in HTTPS our legacy API endpoint is exposed to the AngularJS application and we get a CORS error. The CORS error is expected as the legacy API should never have requests made to it directly from the application.

What I believe is happening is that with `enforceHTTPS` set to true on the router the request from the new API to the legacy API is being redirected which in turn exposes the legacy API endpoint. As the redirect only happens with `enforceHTTPS` set to true I think the block of code in the nginx template below is doing the redirect...

https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/v1.9.0/router/image/templates/nginx.conf#L303

We are running v1.9.0 of the Deis platform on AWS using a Cephless install. SSL termination is done on the Deis router as later on we require the use of sockets which AWS ELBs do not support.

All of out API endpoints only ever make HTTPS calls so there should never be a cause for the Deis router to action a redirect.

issue_title: Proposal: update etcdctl on all new CoreOS hosts
issue_body:
Currently, we update etcdctl on CoreOS hosts _if_ one has opted into the graceful shutdown unit.

What if we just update etcdctl on _all_ new CoreOS clusters instead?  This would significantly aid troubleshooting efforts since newer versions expose things like `member list` and `cluster-health`.

This shouldn't break anything.  Any containerized component would continue to use the older etcdctl 0.4.6 to ensure backwards compatibility with older clusters.

The inverse of `globus login`.
This can't touch the consent in Globus Auth, but it should invalidate all of your tokens by revoking them, and scrub them from the config file.

issue_title: Globus Connect Personal UUID
issue_body:
GCP has a file containing the UUID of its endpoint. Unix installs it goes in `~/.globusonline/lta/client-id.txt` and on Windows it goes in `%LocalAppData%\Globus Connect\client-id.txt`.

So we do have the ability to provide a command to show the UUID to a user which I think we certainly want to do.  The question is really, should this just be implemented directly in the CLI or should we also add SDK methods?

from globus_sdk.authorizers import BasicAuthorizer

# UnicodeDecodeError: 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe3 in position 11: ordinal not in range(128)

issue_title: Require [general] section header in config, be standard INI
issue_body:
Although it originally seemed like a nice bit of user-friendliness to not strictly require a section heading for the `[general]` section, it's causing grief in the CLI already ( globus/globus-cli#12 ).

Standard INI format requires that all sections have headers, so we should just move to that.
It does mean that any SDK users will get parsing errors if we do the upgrade harshly.

We should update tokens.globus.org , then tell people on the developer listhost that we're changing this and they should update.
Since the current parser tolerates full INI files, no one will have issues who listens to us.

After that's done, we're still in beta, so I think it will be safe to just drop the special parsing code.

@bda , thoughts?

issue_title: MRS to DMRS conversion bug
issue_body:
Given an MRS for sentence 'Nonetheless, it is worth watching.' produced by ACE:
```
[ LTOP: h0
INDEX: e2 [ e SF: prop TENSE: pres MOOD: indicative PROG: - PERF: - ]
RELS: < [ "_nonetheless_a_1_rel"<0:12> LBL: h1 ARG0: e4 [ e SF: prop TENSE: untensed MOOD: indicative PROG: - PERF: - ] ARG1: h5 ]
 [ pron_rel<13:15> LBL: h6 ARG0: x3 [ x PERS: 3 NUM: sg GEND: n PRONTYPE: std_pron ] ]
 [ pronoun_q_rel<13:15> LBL: h7 ARG0: x3 RSTR: h8 BODY: h9 ]
 [ "_worth_a_1_rel"<19:24> LBL: h10 ARG0: i11 ARG1: h12 ]
 [ "_watch_v_1_rel"<25:34> LBL: h10 ARG0: e13 [ e SF: prop-or-ques TENSE: untensed MOOD: indicative PROG: + PERF: - ] ARG1: i14 ARG2: x3 ] >
HCONS: < h0 qeq h1 h5 qeq h10 h8 qeq h6 h12 qeq h10 > ]
```
conversion using pyDelphin does not produce the correct result:
```
<dmrs cfrom="-1" cto="-1">
<node cfrom="0" cto="12" nodeid="10000"><realpred lemma="nonetheless" pos="a" sense="1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="e" mood="indicative" perf="-" prog="-" sf="prop" tense="untensed" /></node>
<node cfrom="13" cto="15" nodeid="10001"><gpred>pron_rel</gpred><sortinfo cvarsort="x" gend="n" num="sg" pers="3" prontype="std_pron" /></node>
<node cfrom="13" cto="15" nodeid="10002"><gpred>pronoun_q_rel</gpred><sortinfo /></node>
<node cfrom="19" cto="24" nodeid="10003"><realpred lemma="worth" pos="a" sense="1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="i" /></node>
<node cfrom="25" cto="34" nodeid="10004"><realpred lemma="watch" pos="v" sense="1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="e" mood="indicative" perf="-" prog="+" sf="prop-or-ques" tense="untensed" /></node>
<link from="0" to="10000"><rargname /><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10000" to="10003"><rargname>ARG1</rargname><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10002" to="10001"><rargname>RSTR</rargname><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10003" to="10003"><rargname>ARG1</rargname><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10003" to="10004"><rargname /><post>EQ</post></link>
<link from="10004" to="10001"><rargname>ARG2</rargname><post>NEQ</post></link>
</dmrs>
```
Note the link ARG1 from 10003 to 10003, and EQ link from 10003 to 10004. 

In contrast, a version of pyDelphin forked on Nov 4, 2014 does not encounter this problem, producing correct DMRS:

```
<dmrs cfrom="-1" cto="-1">
<node cfrom="0" cto="12" nodeid="10000"><realpred lemma="nonetheless" pos="a" sense="1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="e" mood="indicative" perf="-" prog="-" sf="prop" tense="untensed" /></node>
<node cfrom="13" cto="15" nodeid="10001"><gpred>pronoun_q_rel</gpred><sortinfo /></node>
<node cfrom="13" cto="15" nodeid="10002"><gpred>pron_rel</gpred><sortinfo cvarsort="x" gend="n" num="sg" pers="3" prontype="std_pron" /></node>
<node cfrom="19" cto="24" nodeid="10003"><realpred lemma="worth" pos="a" sense="1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="i" /></node>
<node cfrom="25" cto="34" nodeid="10004"><realpred lemma="watch" pos="v" sense="1" /><sortinfo cvarsort="e" mood="indicative" perf="-" prog="+" sf="prop-or-ques" tense="untensed" /></node>
<link from="0" to="10000"><rargname /><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10000" to="10004"><rargname>ARG1</rargname><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10001" to="10002"><rargname>RSTR</rargname><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10003" to="10004"><rargname>ARG1</rargname><post>H</post></link>
<link from="10004" to="10002"><rargname>ARG2</rargname><post>NEQ</post></link>
</dmrs>
```

The conversion is done by loading the MRS string as simpleMRS and then dumping it as dmrx. You can see full code here if needed: https://github.com/matichorvat/pydmrs/blob/master/mrs_to_dmrs/mrs_to_dmrs.py

issue_title: QUESTION: Does building pure python modules w/ conda require setuptools?
issue_body:
This weekend I've been reading up on [conda](http://conda.pydata.org/docs/) and the [python packaging user guide](https://python-packaging-user-guide.readthedocs.org/en/latest/) because I have a [simple pure python project](https://github.com/jrsmith3/physicalproperty) that depends on `numpy`. It seemed to me that distributing/installing this project via `conda` was better than `pip` due to this dependency. 

One thing on which I'm still not clear: `conda` will install a python package from a recipe in `build.sh`, but it seems like `build.sh` just ends up calling `python setup.py install` for most python packages.

So even if I want to distribute/install my python package with `conda`, I still end up depending on `setuptools` (or `distutils`) for the actual installation, correct? I was unable to find a `conda` utility analogous to `setuptools`; am I missing something?

issue_title: ability to have conda reside in private environment
issue_body:
In this #3068, we add the ability for conda to reside in the `_conda` environment. Don't worry, conda will still work when installed in the root environment. This is achieved by having a Boolean config variable `config.conda_in_root` (and `config.conda_private` for convenience inience) in order preserve old behavior.  Also, because the `_conda` environment is considered private, it is not treated special in any way. That is, you can install or remove any package from `_conda`, even `conda remove -n _conda conda`.

To do (all these items imply that `config.conda_private` is `True`):

- [ ] `conda update conda` will have to somehow alias to to `conda update -n _conda conda`. Should regular usage of conda try self updates too?
- [ ] think about disallowing `conda install -n root conda`, as it would overwrite the symlinks.
- [ ] think about `conda install conda-build`. Right now I'm thinking that any package which lists conda as a dependency gets installed into `_conda`. Note that conda build works out of the box already when you `conda install -n _conda conda-build`. Think about implications for activate and deactivate.

The good news is that because of `config.conda_in_root`, we can for a long time (maybe even indefinitely) develop on a single conda codebase which supports both the classic root install of conda, and the new `_conda` environment.

What this means for the Anaconda (hopefully 4.2) installer, is that the anaconda and only its dependencies (which conda is already not) will be installed in the root environment, and conda in the `_conda` environment. The resulting installer will not be larger than before, and also not take up more space once installed, because of the use of hard-links. This will make Anaconda and Miniconda very similar, one has a populated root environment and the other does not.

issue_title: conda create mixing vc10 and vc14 packages
issue_body:
As of recently, matplotlib builds on appveyor have been failing, specifically with Python 3.4. The proximate cause is this link failure:

```
Creating library build\temp.win-amd64-3.4\Release\src\_png.lib and object build\temp.win-amd64-3.4\Release\src\_png.exp
png.lib(pngerror.c.obj) : error LNK2019: unresolved external symbol __report_rangecheckfailure referenced in function png_fixed_error
png.lib(pngwutil.c.obj) : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol __report_rangecheckfailure
z.lib(inftrees.obj) : error LNK2001: unresolved external symbol __report_rangecheckfailure
png.lib(pngerror.c.obj) : error LNK2019: unresolved external symbol __imp___acrt_iob_func referenced in function png_chunk_error
png.lib(pngerror.c.obj) : error LNK2019: unresolved external symbol __imp___stdio_common_vfprintf referenced in function _vfprintf_l
build\lib.win-amd64-3.4\matplotlib\_png.pyd : fatal error LNK1120: 3 unresolved externals
error: command 'c:\\Program Files (x86)\\Microsoft Visual Studio 10.0\\VC\\Bin\\amd64\\link.exe' failed with exit status 1120
```

I surmise this is because of mixing vc10 and vc14 libraries. The following is part of the output of `conda create -q -n test-environment python=%PYTHON_VERSION% pip setuptools numpy python-dateutil freetype=2.6 msinttypes "tk=8.5" pyparsing pytz tornado "libpng>=1.6.21,<1.7" "zlib=1.2" "cycler>=0.10" nose mock sphinx` (where PYTHON_VERSION is 3.4)>

```
The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED:
...
    freetype:        2.6.3-vc14_1     conda-forge [vc14]
    imagesize:       0.7.1-py34_0     conda-forge
    jinja2:          2.8-py34_1       conda-forge
    libpng:          1.6.28-vc14_0    conda-forge [vc14]
...
    python:          3.4.5-2          conda-forge
...
    vc:              14-0             conda-forge
    vs2010_runtime:  10.00.40219.1-2  defaults   
    vs2015_runtime:  14.0.25420-0     conda-forge
...
```

I'm not at all clear on the vc10 and vc14 distinction, but I suspect you wouldn't want to mix different runtime versions. The best documentation that I found via Google is https://github.com/conda/conda/wiki/VC-features which indicates that py34 should correspond to vc10.

Is there something we could do in the `conda create` command that would prevent installing any vc14 packages?

AppVeyor config file: https://github.com/matplotlib/matplotlib/blob/master/appveyor.yml
Output of a failing build: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/mdboom/matplotlib/build/1.0.4098/job/gwqemmaetj9u3wt7

issue_title: users.py encryption/format object uniformity
issue_body:
Ref: https://github.com/arista-eosplus/pyeapi/blob/develop/pyeapi/api/users.py#L134

There's a lack of uniformity in the module concerning format and encryption (as I can tell).

When you run .get(name) you get an object like:

```
>>> users.get('gertie')
{'privilege': '8', 'nopassword': False, 'secret': '$1$MQZr6HMw$JxamK.dYxewxYdawMgoZU/', 'role': 'network-admin', 'format': '5'}
```

Where format is the cli-ish value of the secret. The difference is in the create* methods.  They use a param named encryption that abstracts the cli keys to cleartext, md5, sha5.  Wouldn't it be nice to change the output of get() to return a format key in the style of encryption?

issue_title: Bulk Index timeout
issue_body:
passing `params=dict(request_timeout=100)` as
```
.bulk(..., params=dict(request_timeout=100))
```
works but when doing chunks it doesn't
```
.bulk(..., chunk_size=100, max_chunk_bytes=100000, params=dict(request_timeout=100))
```
because `params` pass to `elasticsearch.helpers.streaming_bulk` as `**kwargs` are then passed all the way down to `elasticsearch.transport.Transport#perform_request` and `kwargs` gets mutated by `timeout = params.pop('request_timeout', None)` which results in subsequents requests to be missing some parameters.

issue_title: indices.exists() return exceptions instead boolean
Bug: termvectors method doesn't work
issue_body:
Running Elasticsearch v 1.5.2, using version 1.9.0 of this library

The termvectors method returns a TransportError ("No handler found for uri") because it is using the syntax introduced by ES versions >=2.0 (_termvectors) instead of that used by ES 1.0 versions (_termvector).  Should be an easy fix.

Hi guys,

I found in docker container your lib can't access es by domain name directly, so I have to convert domain to ip address by "socket.gethostbyname".

This issue maybe relates to https://stackoverflow.com/questions/40347726/python-3-5-asyncio-and-aiohttp-errno-101-network-is-unreachable

Please check

issue_title: `client_factory` kwarg bug in KnackConversion branch
issue_body:
When using the `custom_command()` method to register a command, the client_factory being used is taken from the `command_type` kwarg. 
The client_factory should instead be taken from the `custom_command_type`

issue_title: [Install] Download tab completion script even when user turns down tab completion
issue_body:
Currently, if you run the installer and answer "no" to whether you want to enable tab completion, it skips downloading the script, so that if you later change your mind, you can't simply follow the directions on the repo to enable it. 

I'd recommend downloading the script regardless and then simply don't run it if they answer "no". That way they can more easily opt-in at a later time.

issue_title: Implement generic wait command
issue_body:
Waiting for a resource to reach a given state is a fairly common operation. See #1168 for one example, other examples include waiting for a VM to be in state running etc. 

We should be able to automate the polling of the state through a generic wait command since there is a fair amount of commonality between all scenarios where the state can be determined by examining the value of an attribute of the object. 

For a "strongly typed" version of wait:

`az vm wait  -g rg -n davm --running`

For waiting for a resource to exist:

`az vm wait -g rg -n davm --exists`

For waiting for an arbitrary property to have a specific value:

`az network lb wait --property provisioningState=provisioned`

Implementation details can vary (one can extend ability use --query to include anything that returns a truth:y value for example) but hopefully the examples conveys some of the possibilities here...

issue_title: Azure Container Services missing from SDK
issue_body: The other SDKs supply a "composite swagger" flag in their config file (here: https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/blob/master/swagger_to_sdk_config.json#L33) which picks up both the compute and container services files.  That flag is missing in the python SDK (node has it).

issue_title: contact/mouse picking constraints on long chains of btMultiBody can create instability
Null Space Inverse Kinematics has problems with fixed joints
issue_body:
I believe the issue found and fixed in Inverse Dynamics with fixed joints (https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/issues/1379) may also be present in Null Space Inverse Kinematics.

In particular, `lowerLimits`, `upperLimits`, `jointRanges`, `restPoses` are expected to have lengths equal to the number of joints by `calculateInverseKinematics` : see e.g.  https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/master/examples/pybullet/pybullet.c#L8120

But once passed to bullet, it looks like the number of dofs is expected instead: e.g. https://github.com/bulletphysics/bullet3/blob/abeae7e1e7e23d5ba8e840e98b46470d4162a73b/examples/SharedMemory/PhysicsClientC_API.cpp#L4001

Unfortunately padding the arrays until they are length numJoints (as suggested in the previous issue) doesn't seem to fix this one.

I am still finding my way around the code base so apologies if I've read this wrong. Thanks again for your time and help!

issue_title: log prob computed incorrectly within _decode_map
issue_body:
It seems like the log probabilities are computed incorrectly when using MAP decoding. In particular, the log probabilities that are returned are positive, which should be impossible.

The problem seems trivial to fix. `posteriors` are not in the log domain, so all that needs to be changed is to modify `logprob = np.max(posteriors, axis=1).sum()` to read `logprob = np.log(np.max(posteriors, axis=1)).sum()`

```def _decode_map(self, X):
        _, posteriors = self.score_samples(X)
        logprob = np.max(posteriors, axis=1).sum() # posteriors are not in log domain
        state_sequence = np.argmax(posteriors, axis=1)
        return logprob, state_sequence
```

issue_title: HTTP requests pipelining in ClientSession
issue_body:
Does Aiohttp 2.0 support pipelining ? I know, that NGINX HTTP parser supports pipelining. But what about Aiohttp ? Does it handle the case :

1. Request 1 parsed, and is awaiting it's coroutine A to complete
2. Request 2 parsed, and is awaiting it's coroutine B to complete
3. Coroutine B completed. But it's response must not be written to the connection, until A's response is written.

In general, for any TCP-connection (used as HTTP), it is possible to have many coroutines that handle requests simultaneously, and their answers must be strictly ordered. It seems, we should have some queue of responses associated with TCP-connection.

Also, we should be able to specify if pipelining should be used. It have many corner-cases which may be not expected. The same for ClientSession. Should it issue piplined requests or not. In both cases pipelining shuold turned off by default (at least for now).

issue_title: body content length measured in characters instead of bytes
issue_body:
## Long story short

Content-Length can be miscalculated if multipart body contains special characters (represented by more htan one bytes). I think the following line causes the trouble, since it uses the length of a string instead of the respective byte array.
https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/payload.py#L243

## Steps to reproduce

```
import asyncio
import aiohttp
from aiohttp import ClientSession
from io import StringIO

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
session = ClientSession(loop=loop)

data = {'asd123': StringIO('ű' * 5000)}
async def x():
    await session.post('http://localhost:2000', data=data)
a = loop.run_until_complete(x())
```

This program results in the following http query:
```

issue_title: __optionsclass__ of sublcass defaults back to ModelOptions
issue_body:
Hi, I am not sure if this is on purpose or not, but when defining a model with a custom `__optionsclass__`, subclasses of that model go back to using `ModelOptions` instead.

An example might make this easier to explain:

```
from schematics.models import Model, ModelOptions


class MyModelOptions(ModelOptions):
    pass


class BaseModel(Model):

    __optionsclass__ = MyModelOptions


class DerivedModel(BaseModel):
    pass


b = BaseModel()
d = DerivedModel()

>>> print(b._options.__class__)
<class '__main__.MyModelOptions'>

>>> print(d._options.__class__)
<class 'schematics.models.ModelOptions'>
```

I would naturally expect `DerivedModel` instances to use `MyModelOptions` like their parent does.

If this is a bug I am happy to provide a PR. If this is correct behavior I'll work around it with a custom meta class.


Thanks.

issue_title: Batchrunner report creation error when values are one of dict/list/ndarray
issue_body:
in batchrunner.py:
`ordered[param] = self.fixed_parameters[param]`

When values are a dict/list/np.ndarray, the above line throws the error:
ValueError: Length of values does not match length of index

This is because pandas interprets these datatypes as multiple values to fill a column with.

I used the following solution:
```
val = self.fixed_parameters[param]
if (type(val) == dict) or (type(val) == np.ndarray) or (type(val) == list):
    ordered[param] = str(val)
else:
    ordered[param] = val
```

It does the job for me, but it doesn't account for potential other datatypes that may have the same problem.  So not a very clean solution.  Any ideas how to handle this better?

issue_title: node.slaveoutput has no key 'environment' when used with xdist and cygwin
issue_body:
I know, this is a horrendous configuration; it's what we're working with.

On line 73 of `pytest_html/plugin.py` the key `environment` is not present in the`node.slaveoutput`. As the title says, this condition is reached when executing pytest with xdist over multiple ssh hosts, that themselves are running ssh daemons with cygwin. I've been able to get around this using:

```
 73         if 'environment' not in node.slaveoutput:
 74             node.slaveoutput['environment'] = None
```

But I am certain causes issues in other circumstances, e.g. when trying to access the `environment` attribute when generating reports. I could instead simply add 'Not Found'... Would you accept a PR with this? I can also give the xdist guys a heads up.

issue_title: util tests are defaulting to "passed"
issue_body:
The assertions made in the functional tests for the util endpoints are defaulting to "passed" because their assertion has been placed inside of the `with ... exception` block.

This causes the exception to be raised and caught by pytest, but since the assertion is coded inside of the exception block (after the exception is raised) the assertion never happens and the test succeeds by default

issue_title: Calling 3rd party DLL that makes calls out to the internet from .Net core fails proxy authentication despite configuring or coding the required settings
issue_body:
I have created a .Net Core 2.0 Web.API that will relay a request to a 3rd party provider via their .Net 4.0 DLL which in turn handles the communication with their Web API and returns the results.

In a simple .Net 4 application this all works perfectly and when I run my Web.API and make calls to it using a direct internet connection again it all works perfectly.

However, if it is run connecting to our work network which uses a proxy I get 407 Proxy Authentication Required. Fair enough all I need do is specify that I want to use the default credentials and it should all work. So A bit of digging around had given me several possible solutions. Unfortunately under .Net Core 2.0 they don't work.

I have tried deploying to my local machine and specifying in the web.Config:

`<system.net>
  <defaultProxy useDefaultCredentials="true" />
</system.net>`

I have also tried adding this configuration to the machine.config (which seemed a bit overkill) again no joy.

I have also tried adding to my code in my Web API:

`System.Net.WebRequest.DefaultWebProxy.Credentials = System.Net.CredentialCache.DefaultNetworkCredentials;
`

both before and after instantiating a reference to the 3rd party DLL and still when the vendors DLL makes its call to their Web API, via HTTP or HTTPS I get back 407 Proxy Authentication Required.

In an attempt to prove that I was not going mad I stripped the core of the work out to a standard .Net application to create basic a test harness and applying either of the solutions to that program it all works as expected. I have also created a separate .Net 4 DLL to act as a wrapper to the 3rd party DLL and in its constructor put in the above code to set the credentials in an attempt to isolate the effect to that environment. Again, this works perfectly under a simple test application but fails under the .Net Core Web.API.

Putting fiddler into the equation to watch the communication with the working application you can see the initial request get denied and proxy authentication get requested, the authentication then gets passed back and the actual request then gets through and the result passed back.

But in the .Net core Web.API I have developed the request simply goes out, hits the proxy authentication request, but then no credentials get pushed back to get things going and the conversation terminates.

This is the first time that I have ever worked with .Net Core so I may be missing something that I need to do, but from my perspective it looks like when I set-up the environment in my application for proxy authentication that same environment/settings are not being passed through to the DLL's environment and so I can't handle the request properly.

So I need some way of setting up or injecting the environment for the 3rd party DLL to ensure that it knows what to do with the authentication request via System.Net.WebRequest.DefaultWebProxy or similar.

So far I have tried running the code in a completely different thread and tried creating a completely separate .Net standard DLL to act as a wrapper, inside that DLL I then use code to force the System.Net.WebRequest.DefaultWebProxy.Credentials to use the default network credentials. Plugging either of these into my test harness they work but as soon as they are put into the .Net Core Web API they fail in exactly the same way.

issue_title: Dllimport does not find libraries outside of system default (Linux)
issue_body:
when doing PInvoke call, like
[DllImport("mylib")]
        private static extern int some_external_func();
the dotnet does not find library, unless in its some system default (like /usr/lib)
If to explicitly state the actual library name like "libmylib.so.0.0.0" it will find it but program crashes, if the "mylib" will use some other library (otside of "default" location), then error message:
["dotnet: undefined symbol ] will appear
ldconfig is finding the libraries correctly, using ld.so.conf and stating custom library folder.

Temporary solution was to add LD_LIBRARY_PATH to the running environment  pointing to the library folder, which should not be necessary if the ldconfig lists those libraries already.

other solution is to handload all the used libraries which will lose all the point of using .net core

issue_title: How to run tests against IL Linked assemblies?
issue_body:
The IL Linker announced in #915 is fantastic.
I'm seeing a 71% reduction in final size!

The [Linker Instructions](https://github.com/dotnet/core/blob/master/samples/linker-instructions.md) mention that the final executables need to be tested after linking.

> Linking only happens during publish, and therefore the linked app needs to be tested after publish, not just after build.

This makes complete sense, but I can't find any information on how to run my tests with `dotnet test` against an already compiled executable. Is there a way? If so, can this information (or a link to it) be added to the linker instructions?

issue_title: Suggest donating to Let's Encrypt after successful issuance
issue_body: We should ask people to donate to Let's Encrypt after successful issuance of a certificate through our client. Maybe present 1-2 sentences and a link to where they can donate. It should be easy to disable this via command-line flag (or some other mechanism).

issue_title: Warn when running from a different host than authorizing
issue_body:
People may decide to run, e.g. the manual authenticator from their laptop rather than from the server. This will work fine. However, the Let's Encrypt privacy policy states that we will retain and publish IP addresses associated with ACME validation requests, including requestor IP. This is unremarkable when running the client on the same machine where a cert will be deployed. When running the client on a personal machine, this behavior will be unexpected to anyone who hasn't read the privacy policy.

We should do a check that the resolved names being authorized point at the current machine. If they don't we should provide a notice to the user that their IP address will be published, along with a link to the privacy policy.

issue_title: Client consumer taking a long time to subscribe when run alongside other consumers
issue_body:
I'm running with python 3.6.2, librdkafka 0.11.1, and confluent-kafka 0.11.0.

My application can run in one of two ways. In the first configuration, it uses two high level producers to send two types of jobs on two different topics. When those jobs are completed, messages get sent back on an additional two topics which are subscribed to by two additional high level consumers. These producers and consumers are run inside of processes created by python's multiproccessing.Process. When the consumers are created, they use
```
consumer.subscribe([topic], on_assign=reset_offsets)
```
and
```
def reset_offsets(consumer, partitions):
  self._partition_count = len(partitions)
  for p in partitions:
    current_offsets = consumer.get_watermark_offsets(p)
    p.offset = current_offsets[1]
  consumer.assign(partitions)
  status['assigned'] = True
```
to ignore old messages and to set the status flag when the consumer is subscribed. This flag is used to indicate when the producers can start sending out jobs. In the first configuration, subscription is relatively quick and happens after a few seconds.

In the second configuration, a consumer runs in the main process which listens for job requests from an API. When a request comes in, a function is called which spawns the additional consumers and producers as described above. However, in this second configuration, job subscription takes much longer (about 1 or 2 minutes), and I'm wondering why this happens. I've tried looking at the logs, but I didn't see anything useful or any glaring error messages. Are these consumers interacting with each other or sharing resources?

The consumer and producer are using SSL to connect in both application configurations, but beyond that the only optional config option that I set is 'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'. I should be able to provide client logs if needed.

issue_title: confluent-kafka-python resiliency to network errors
issue_body:
How resilient is python confluent-kafka to network errors? I am asking because I noticed two problems when the network between the python kafka client and the kafka brokers is interrupted for 10 minutes. 
I have a message producer and a message consumer (code below). 
The producer produces messages with a sequence number, the consumer receives those messages and print them on a file.

This is what I see when the network is turned of for around 10 mins and then turned on again: 
- On the producer I see message delivery errors (code=_MSG_TIMED_OUT) reported by the on_delivery callback. I expected these messages to be flushed when the network was up again, but all the messages produced while the network was down are lost (see second consumer log). I thought the messages produced while the network is down are stored in an internal local queue (no exception is raised by the produce call), and they are all flushed when 1) the network is up again, and 2) a call to the flush method is made. This does not seem to be the case. 
After the network is up again the new messages are delivered without problems.
- On the consumer I see one TIMED_OUT_QUEUE exception ~90 seconds after the network is down, and I see no messages received from that moment on, even after the network is up again. Thinking there was a problem with the state of the consumer I tried to reinitialize it (calling the consumer's unsubscribe, close, init and subscribe again) but this did not help. The consumer is able to consume messages again only if I restart the consumer program. The messages produced with the delivery errors are lost.

I am using python confluent-kafka 0.9.2 with librdkafka 0.9.2. The version of the kafka broker is 0.10.0.1.

**Producer Code:**

```
def produce(config, topic_name, retry_interval):
    def on_delivery(error, msg):
        if error is None:
            logger.info("Message delivered: '%s'", msg.value())
        else:
            logger.warning("An error was encountered while delivering a kafka message: %s", error)
    
    producer_config = config.copy()
    producer_config.update({
        'client.id': 'test-producer',
        'api.version.request': True
    })
    
    logger.debug("Initializing producer ...")
    producer = confluent_kafka.Producer(producer_config)
    logger.debug("Producer initialized.")

    n = 1    
    while True:
        try: 
            value = {
                "sequence_no": n
            }
                
            logger.info("Producing message: '%s' ...", value)
            producer.produce(topic_name, json.dumps(value), callback=on_delivery)
            logger.info ("Message produced: '%s' ...", value)
            time.sleep(2)
            logger.debug("Flushing messages ...")
            producer.flush()
            logger.debug("Messages flushed.")
            n += 1
            time.sleep(2)
        except confluent_kafka.KafkaException as e:
            logger.warning("An error was encountered while producing a kafka message: %s", str(e.args[0]))
            logger.warning("Retrying producing kafka message ...")
            time.sleep(retry_interval)
```

**Consumer code:**

```
def consume(config, topic, poll_timeout, retry_interval):
    consumer_config = config.copy()
    consumer_config.update({
        'client.id': 'test-consumer',
        'group.id': 'test-consumer-group',
        'api.version.request': True,
        'default.topic.config': {
            'enable.auto.commit': False,        #default: True
            'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest',    #default: largest            
        }         
    })
    
    logger.debug("Initializing consumer ...")                
    consumer = confluent_kafka.Consumer(consumer_config)
    logger.debug("Consumer initialized.")                
    subscribed = False
    
    while True:
        try:
            if not subscribed:
                consumer.subscribe([topic])
                logger.debug("Consumer subscribed to: %s",  topic)
                subscribed = True
                
            msg = consumer.poll(poll_timeout)           
            if msg is None:
                # poll timed out
                logger.debug("POLL TIMED OUT")
                continue            

            if not msg.error():
                logger.info("Message received: %s", msg.value())
                logger.info("Committing message: %s", msg.value())
                consumer.commit(msg)
                logger.info("Message committed: %s", msg.value())
            else:
                error_code = msg.error().code()
                if error_code == confluent_kafka.KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
                    continue
                 
                logger.error("An error was encountered while consuming kafka messages: %s", str(msg.error()))
                raise confluent_kafka.KafkaException(msg.error())
        except confluent_kafka.KafkaException as e:
            logger.warning("A possibly recoverable error was encountered while consuming kafka messages: %s", str(e.args[0]))
            logger.warning("Retrying consuming kafka messages ...")
            time.sleep(retry_interval)
            
            # reconnect
            if subscribed:
                logger.debug("Unsubscribing consumer ...")                
                consumer.unsubscribe()
                logger.debug("Consumer unsubscribed.")                
                subscribed = False
                
            logger.debug("Closing consumer ...")                
            consumer.close()
            logger.debug("Consumer closed.")                
            logger.debug("Reinitializing consumer ...")                
            consumer = confluent_kafka.Consumer(consumer_config)
            logger.debug("Consumer reinitialized.")
```

issue_title: AvroProducer doesn't serialize the key if it's an empty string
issue_body:
Description
===========

Based on [the code for AvroProducer](https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/blob/master/confluent_kafka/avro/__init__.py#L68)
AvroProducer serializes the key only if `bool(key) == True`, which allows to pass an empty string as a key without the serialization.

This breaks the read using AvroConsumer, because it [tries to deserialize the key if `message.key() is not None`]( https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/blob/master/confluent_kafka/avro/__init__.py#L117)
And the serializer raises the following error [at the length check](https://github.com/confluentinc/confluent-kafka-python/blob/master/confluent_kafka/avro/serializer/message_serializer.py#L208)
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/alfiya/miniconda3/envs/py3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/IPython/core/interactiveshell.py", line 2862, in run_code
    exec(code_obj, self.user_global_ns, self.user_ns)
  File "<ipython-input-20-7566bd90b3a5>", line 1, in <module>
    m=consumer.poll()
  File "/Users/alfiya/miniconda3/envs/py3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/confluent_kafka/avro/__init__.py", line 118, in poll
    decoded_key = self._serializer.decode_message(message.key())
  File "/Users/alfiya/miniconda3/envs/py3/lib/python3.6/site-packages/confluent_kafka/avro/serializer/message_serializer.py", line 209, in decode_message
    raise SerializerError("message is too small to decode")
```


How to reproduce
================

```python
import avro.schema
import confluent_kafka.avro

producer = confluent_kafka.avro.AvroProducer(
    {
        'api.version.request': True,
        'compression.codec': 'gzip',
        'default.topic.config': {'request.required.acks': 1},
        'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9093',
        'schema.registry.url': 'http://localhost:8081',
    }, 
    default_key_schema=avro.schema.Parse('{"type": "string"}'), 
    default_value_schema=avro.schema.Parse('{"type": "string"}')
)
producer.produce(topic='bug', value='not empty', key='')

consumer = confluent_kafka.avro.AvroConsumer({
    'group.id': 'mygroup', 
    'api.version.request': True,
    'default.topic.config': {'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'},
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost:9093',
    'schema.registry.url': 'http://localhost:8081'
    
})
consumer.subscribe(['bug'])
consumer.poll()
```
At `consumer.poll()` the error above should be raised:

Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [x] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`): `confluent_kafka.version()`: ('0.11.0', 720896), `confluent_kafka.libversion()`: ('0.11.3', 721919)
 - [x] Apache Kafka broker version: 0.10.2.1
 - [x] Client configuration: `{...}` provided in 'How to reproduce'

issue_title: Provide an easy way to preload some packages that exist on pypi.python.org
issue_body:
One of the most important advantages of having a private pypiserver is that you decouple yourself from the dependency of pypi.python.org which can go down or receive changes that may impact you.

We need a documented way to do something like:

* upload latest version of package `foobar` from pypi.python.org to our internal server
* upload foobar==1.2.3 from pypi to our internal server
* remove package version from internal server

Please note that fallback feature cannot be used because it does affect all packages. We need to be able to control what and when gets updated.

The workaround at this moment is to manually download these files from pypi and to put them on disk, which is an ugly manual process.

I'm trying to wrap a `zip` command with multiple files as input.  The input to the job is just an array of source files.  The goal is to have the source files at the root of the resulting zip with no sub-directories.

By default when the command is rendered it passes the input files with some sort of temporary path like `/private/var/folders/9h/j5_q_5hx4511j4blrvs9vc6c0000gq/T/tmpSJzLPX/stg767105dc-13b4-403d-8663-4c6c6133d663/hello.txt`  This results in a zip file that contains a long nested folder.

issue_title: Use multiple exception classes
issue_body:
When catching exceptions, users probably want to see the exception type and write code to handle it.

Example:

```python
import sys

try:
    f = open('myfile.txt')
    s = f.readline()
    i = int(s.strip())
except IOError as e:
    print "I/O error({0}): {1}".format(e.errno, e.strerror)
except ValueError:
    print "Could not convert data to an integer."
except:
    print "Unexpected error:", sys.exc_info()[0]
    raise
```

issue_title: raster_handler Breaks Compatibility with Python 3
issue_body:
Hi guys,

I was recently contacted by Descartes Labs recruiter, Mike Jacobs, for an opening as Python Developer. While doing some research on your company, I somehow stumbled upon this repository. So naturally, I started playing around with your code (hope you don't mind). In the process I found some minor bugs in the file ./descarteslabs/scripts/parser/raster.py that break compatibility with Python 3.

First, on line 50,

```python
for filename, data in response['files'].iteritems():
```

is an issue because Python 3 no longer supports the 'iteritems' method for dictionaries; this method has actually replaced the 'items' method. So I propose replacing 'iteritems' with 'items', as this will run in Python 2 and Python 3 with only slightly different side effects. Otherwise, you can test for Python 3  using `if sys.version_info > (3, 0)` and then call the appropriate method.

Also, lines 51-52 break Python 3 compatibility. The code

```python
    with open(filename, "w") as f:
        f.write(data)
```

raises an error because the variable 'data' is a bytes object (read from a binary file) and `f.write(data)` attempts to write the bytes object to a file which was _not_ opened in binary mode. In Python 2 this wasn't a problem on Unix machines because Unix treats text and binary files the same. However, Python 3 aims to be more platform-independent and requires you to open a file in binary mode if writing bytes objects to the file (see https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/functions.html#open). So my proposal is simply replacing the mode "w" with "wb". 

Anyways, I hope you find this helpful and not too intrusive or annoying :grimacing:. I'm happy to lend a hand and submit a pull request, if you'd like. Let me know if there's anything else I can help with!

Best regards,
Ryan Arredondo

All across dispatch delete buttons work with a single click, deleting entries with no confirmation.

I suggest a DeleteButton component, that takes the delete method as a prop, but instead of running it onclick, it should open a popover, modal, or something to that affect, asking for confirmation of the delete.

issue_title: processor parameter for fields
issue_body:
Please suppose this only for desrialization(load):
```python
class MySchema(Schema):
    first = fields.String(processor=[unicode.strip, filter1, filter2])
    second = fields.String(processor=[unicode.strip, unicode.lower, filter1, filter3])
    third = fields.String(processor=[formatter(indent=4), filter2])
    fourth = fields.String(processor=formatter(clean=True))
```
Processor or collection of processors must be callable. for example: filter1..3 are function and formatter is a callable object. I often used these processors.
I added this feature to the my global Schema by:
```python
@Schema.preprocessor
def preprocessor(schema, input_data):
    for name in input_data:
        processor = schema.fields[name].metadata.get('processor')
        if processor:
            processors = processor if is_collection(processor) else [processor]
            # piping
            for func in processors:
                input_data[name] = func(input_data[name])
    return input_data
```
As you can see i could not use custom Field because processors may vary for each field, however i would like to have pure fields rather than customs!

?

issue_title: Resource identifiers and relationships
issue_body:
Currently, marshmallow-jsonapi doesn't have a way to easily generate [resource identifier objects][rio], typically used for relationship requests.

# Example
## Fetch resource

```
GET /articles/1
```

```json
{
    "data": {
        "type": "articles",
        "id": "1",
        "attributes": {"title": "This is a title."},
        "relationships": {
            "author": {
                "links": {
                    "self": "/articles/1/relationships/author",
                    "related": "/articles/1/author"
                },
                "data": {"id": "10", "type": "people"}
            }
        }
    },
    "links": {"self": "/articles/1"}
}
```

## Fetch relationship

This request isn't possible directly using marshmallow-jsonapi:

```
GET /articles/1/relationships/author
```

```json
{
    "data": {"id": "10", "type": "people"},
    "links": {
        "self": "/articles/1/relationships/author",
        "related": "/articles/1/author"
    }
}
```

I've been trying to think of a nice API to do this, and came up with the following:

```python
schema = AuthorSchema(
    resource_identifier=True,
    self_url='/articles/1/relationships/author',
    related_url='/articles/1/author')
```

Setting `resource_identifier=True` prevents `relationships`, `attributes`, and `links` keys from appearing in a resource object.

`self_url` and `related_url` allow overriding the top level `links` object, since `AuthorSchema` doesn't know about the article relationship.

## Fetch related resource

```
GET /articles/1/author
```

```json
{
    "data": {
        "type": "people",
        "id": "10",
        "attributes": {
            "name": "Jane Smith"
        },
        "links": "/people/10"
    },
    "links": {"self": "/articles/1/author"}
}
```

In this case we have to have to use

```python
schema = AuthorSchema(self_url='/articles/1/author')
```

**Is this a good addition to the API?** I can open a PR.

Maybe the flask extension should support the following:

```python
from flask import request

@app.route('/articles/<int:article_id>/relationships/author')
def article_author_relationship(article_id):
    schema = AuthorSchema(
        resource_identifier=True,
        self_view=request.endpoint,
        self_view_kwargs=request.view_args,
        related_view='article_author',
        related_view_kwargs=dict(article_id=article_id))

    ...
```

The flask extensions `Schema` class would just call `url_for` in `__init__` and pass to `BaseSchema`.

[rio]: http://jsonapi.org/format/#document-resource-identifier-objects

issue_title: The Serial class in the .NET/Mono (IronPython) backend does not implement the _reconfigure_port method
issue_body:
There is no _reconfigure_port(self) function in serialcli.py but it is expected by the parent class SerialBase in serialutil.py. This causes SerialBase to generate the message *AttributeError: 'Serial' object has no attribute '_reconfigure_port'*.

The SerialBase class implemented in serialutil.py expects _reconfigure_port(self) to be implemented by subclasses. This is not explicitely stated in the module, but it is clear from examining the code.
For example, from serialutil.py:

```python
@baudrate.setter
def baudrate(self, baudrate):
    """\
    Change baud rate. It raises a ValueError if the port is open and the
    baud rate is not possible. If the port is closed, then the value is
    accepted and the exception is raised when the port is opened.
    """
    try:
        b = int(baudrate)
    except TypeError:
        raise ValueError("Not a valid baudrate: %r" % (baudrate,))
    else:
        if b <= 0:
            raise ValueError("Not a valid baudrate: %r" % (baudrate,))
        self._baudrate = b
        if self.is_open:
            self._reconfigure_port()
```

The Serial(SerialBase) class implemented in serialcli.py has _configurePort(self) 
but _configure_port(self) does not exist:

```python
def _reconfigurePort(self):
    """Set communication parameters on opened port."""
    if not self._port_handle:
        raise SerialException("Can only operate on a valid port handle")
```

PicklingError: Can't pickle <class 'uuid.UUID'>: it's not the same object as uuid.UUID
issue_body:
Hi there, I've had an intermittent issue come up using Cachalot in our production environment (Apache, mod_wsgi, Python 2.7, Django 1.12)

Every few weeks we get this error "Can't pickle <class 'uuid.UUID'>: it's not the same object as uuid.UUID", from deep inside the monkey patch layer of Cachalot.  (Example sentry stack trace below).

The only thing that fixes it is restarting Apache. It'll then go away for a day or two, sometimes a week or two, then reoccur.

`PicklingError
Can't pickle <class 'uuid.UUID'>: it's not the same object as uuid.UUID
cachalot/monkey_patch.py in _get_result_or_execute_query at line 59
    result = execute_query_func()
    if result.__class__ not in ITERABLES and isinstance(result, Iterable):
        result = list(result)
    cache.set(cache_key, (time(), result), cachalot_settings.CACHALOT_TIMEOUT)
    return result
`

Researching this, I've only found 1 other person with the same issue (unresolved) at https://github.com/niwinz/django-redis/issues/319.

Reading up on the pickling error, it seems that if you import a symbol in different ways (i.e. import x x.y versus from x import y) it results in this pickling error.

I looked through all my code to ensure that UUID was being used and imported in a consistent way, checking also how the Django Postgresql code uses it (as I use models.UUIDField for primary keys). Everywhere uses it as such:

`import uuid
...
uuid.UUID`

Except for this place in Cachalot, utils.py

`
from time import time
**from uuid import UUID**

from django import VERSION as django_version
from django.db import connections
from django.db.models import QuerySet
from django.db.models.sql import Query
from django.db.models.sql.where import (
    ExtraWhere, SubqueryConstraint, WhereNode)
from django.utils.six import text_type, binary_type, PY2

from .settings import ITERABLES, cachalot_settings
from .transaction import AtomicCache


class UncachableQuery(Exception):
    pass


class IsRawQuery(Exception):
    pass


CACHABLE_PARAM_TYPES = {
    bool, int, float, Decimal, bytearray, binary_type, text_type, type(None),
    datetime.date, datetime.time, datetime.datetime, datetime.timedelta, **UUID**,
}

`

I suspect this is the source of the issue here. I'm going to try patching this on our project with the following changes to see if it solves the issue (may have to wait 3-4 weeks before declaring it solved given the mean-time between failures).

suggested change below:
`
**import uuid**
...

CACHABLE_PARAM_TYPES = {
    bool, int, float, Decimal, bytearray, binary_type, text_type, type(None),
    datetime.date, datetime.time, datetime.datetime, datetime.timedelta, **uuid.UUID**,
}

`

I am migrating my experimental framework (with basic scheduling) to sacred. 

My framework scheduler might call same experiment multiple times from different machines. In case an experiment is COMPLETED or RUNNING, then a pre-hook exits the experiment (skipping the execution of the main function). Otherwise (when the experiment is DEAD or does not exists), the experiment is launched . Or in other words, it avoids running the same experiment twice.

Is there a way to have a similar functionality in sacred?

issue_title: Stacks with a weight of 0 are shown as receiving 0% traffic
issue_body:
We currently have some stacks where all aliases in Route 53 have 0. I don't know how this happened and that is a different problem anyway. However, `senza traffic` lists all stacks with 0% traffic when in fact they all receive an equal amount of traffic. So for example if one stack has two versions and both have a DNS weight of 0 then senza should say 50% for both.

I think the issue is [here](https://github.com/zalando-stups/senza/blob/2.1.64/senza/traffic.py#L55) but I'm not sure. It seems all stacks with a weight of 0 are filtered out which misses the corner case of all stacks having a 0.

This is probably (i.e. haven't tested it) also a problem for the traffic detection of `senza delete` which [only checks for a Route 53 weight greater than zero](https://github.com/zalando-stups/senza/blob/2.1.64/senza/cli.py#L763). While I'm not mindlessly going around deleting stacks, I'm relying on this feature as a safeguard.

We need to evaluate the performance impact (I assume it's relatively small) and change gluon nlp language models to hybrid blocks for ease of deployment if possible. It should not affect existing pre-trained models since the parameter structure won't change.

issue_body:
For the following code:

```
(set-option :produce-unsat-cores true)
(set-logic QF_UF)

(declare-fun p () Bool)
(declare-fun q () Bool)
(declare-fun r () Bool)

solver returned:

issue_title: Strange define-fun behavior with Int/Real
issue_body:
z3 reports an error for the following:

```smt2
(define-fun fun ( (v_0 Real) ) Bool true)
(assert (fun 1));      ^|^^
(check-sat); ^----------|
```

```smt2
(error "line 2 column 14: unknown function/constant fun")
sat
```

Column `14` is the `1` of `(fun 1)`. I just confirmed it on 792fdb915f7098177e6414e25a59373d3c7b9ca7 (latest at time of writing). But [rise4fun/z3](https://rise4fun.com/Z3/uJab) does not produce any error.

The error goes away if I pass a fraction instead:

```smt2
(define-fun fun ( (v_0 Real) ) Bool true)
(assert (fun (/ 1 1)))
(check-sat)
```

It works the other way around:

```smt2
(define-fun fun ( (v_0 Int) ) Bool true)
(assert (fun (/ 1 1)));^|^
(check-sat); ^^^^^^^----|
```

```smt2
(error "line 2 column 20: unknown function/constant fun")
sat
```

Column `20` is the `)` of `(/ 1 1)`. [Rise4fun/z3](https://rise4fun.com/Z3/6BIVU) does not complain.

Is this expected? I mean, the error is off, but are these scripts supposed to be rejected?

issue_title: Problem with generatiny explanations in Datalog engine
issue_body:
I have tried to coax some explanations out of the muZ datalog engine, but my attempts so far have been unsuccessful. To illustrate the issue, here is a short Python example adapted from the tutorial:

```
from z3 import *

fp = Fixedpoint()
fp.set(engine='datalog')
fp.set('datalog.generate_explanations', True)

a, b, c = Bools('a b c')

fp.register_relation(a.decl(), b.decl(), c.decl())
fp.rule(a,b, name='r1')
fp.rule(b,c, name='r2')
fp.fact(c)
print fp.query(a)
print fp.get_answer()
```

When run using z3 4.5.0 on macOS, it produces the following output:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "datalog.py", line 13, in <module>
    print fp.query(a)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/z3/z3.py", line 6431, in query
    r = Z3_fixedpoint_query(self.ctx.ref(), self.fixedpoint, query.as_ast())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/z3/z3core.py", line 4629, in Z3_fixedpoint_query
    raise Z3Exception(lib().Z3_get_error_msg(a0, err))
z3.z3types.Z3Exception: Rule contains infinite sorts in rule a!expl!2(#0) :- 
 b!expl!1(#1),
 (= (:var 0) (r1 (:var 1))).
```

I also tried previous releases on macOS and on GNU/Linux, but received the same results. I have no idea what to make of it without diving into the source code. Perhaps this is not a bug, but simply the result of my limited understanding. In any case, I would be immensely grateful if you could help with this issue.

issue_title: Semantic change for TIMEOUT option between 4.4.1 and 4.5?
issue_body:
I'm currently trying out [Boogie](https://github.com/boogie-org/boogie) with Z3 4.5 and Boogie currently isn't working because Z3 is behaving differently when it sees

```
(set-option :TIMEOUT 0)
```

Previously Z3 would treat this as there being no timeout. Now Z3 treats this as being no time being allowed for solving which is pretty useless. So now Boogie makes no progress because all solver queries return `unknown` with the reason `canceled`.

I strongly suspect (but haven't checked) that this change is related to this change

https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/commit/a6e1c70eab988de59560c34a4222c99ce80e2feb

which was made in response to an issue I reported a while back ( https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/issues/445 ).

I can work around this in Boogie but this change might need rethinking. Why would anyone ever want to give Z3 no time to solver a query?

issue_title: [CMake] Regeneration of some `.cpp` files are broken
issue_body:
Currently the CMake build system fails to re-generate the `install_tactic.cpp`, `mem_initializer.cpp`, and `gparams_register_modules.cpp` files when the headers that are used to generate these files change.

The reason for this is because CMake is currently unaware that the generation of these files depends on certain header files.

We cannot use shell globing in CMake to fix this because this will fail in the case that new header files are added. The right way to fix this is to change the interface of `z3_add_component()` so that header files that need to be scanned to generate header files are explicitly listed.

When I have the time I'll fix this. This blocks #986.

* Data access: this one, we had already seen during the class: Project Gutenberg now requires a click to access the data, so the automatic remote url load fails.  Users can work around it by manually downloading the files and uploading them back to data8, but that sort of defeats the purpose.

A good alternative suggested by Saul would be to have cached copies of key datasets in a data8 url, and update  the examples to pull from here instead.  This would still show folks the use of the http-based data access (better than storing them as local files) while circumventing remote blocks.  You could add a comment along the lines of:

```
# We will access this remote data
#huck_finn_url = 'http://www.gutenberg.org/cache/epub/76/pg76.txt'
# from a Berkeley cache so the whole class can fetch the data.
huck_finn_url = 'http://berkeley-cache.data8.org/gutenberg/epub/76/pg76.txt'# When working from home with a local copy of the tools, you can try using
# the original address.
```

or similar... There are other solutions (full-on proxy server for all data requests, etc), just wanted to mention the issue as a current blocker so you can consider the options.

issue_title: Deadended states lost when executing part of a binary
issue_body:
Hello,

In case we skip part of a binary and start our simulation manager at a specific function (usually `main`), when we reach the end of this function, the return site is symbolic, thus the warning `Exit state has over 257 possible solutions. Likely unconstrained; skipping.`. This puts the new state into `unconstrained_successors`, however, imho, because of these lines in `manager.py`, we lost the state (it is no more in the `simgr.stashes`):
```
    if not active and not unconstrained and orig:
      always_present['deadended'].append(orig)
```
Is this behaviour intended?

issue_title: Odo 0.4.0 complaining about windows-style line terminators with csv->postgresql
issue_body:
As of 0.4.0, Odo throws a ValueError whenever importing a CSV file with windows style line terminators into PostgreSQL (with error message: "PostgreSQL does not support line terminators other than \n").

However, according to PostgreSQL 9.5 COPY FROM's documentation, "COPY FROM can handle lines ending with newlines, carriage returns, or carriage return/newlines."

Having used Odo 0.3.4 before, I did not have any trouble importing such CSV files.

Therefore, I don't understand why this check and error were introduced?

Sincerely,
Arthur Cheysson

issue_title: Copter: in angle control GUIDED mode the drone keeps rotating on its yaw axis when told not to do so
issue_body:
#### Issue details
In GUIDED mode, using angle control with the `SET_ATTITUDE_TARGET` messages, the drone keeps rotating on its yaw axis.

It will first quickly go to a yaw angle (presumably the one set in the command's quaternion), and then will keep turning at a more or less constant rotation speed.

During another flight we even had a crash right when we passed the vehicle from LOITER to GUIDED, using the included code example with the `yaw_rate` at `2` in the parameters of `send_attitude_target`: the drone leaned very hard (maybe 45°) on both its pitch and roll axes, quickly losing altitude.

The `WP_YAW_BEHAVIOR` was set to `0` (`WP_YAW_BEHAVIOR_NONE`) for all those tests.

I took a couple videos using the example code below to illustrate the problem, I will include them if requested. Same with the logs, will try to find them if requested :smile: 

#### Version
3.4.6

#### Platform
- [ ] All
- [ ] AntennaTracker
- [x] Copter
- [ ] Plane
- [ ] Rover

#### Airframe type
quad

#### Hardware type
Erle Brain 3

### Code

``` python

import math
import time

import dronekit

copter = dronekit.connect('udp:10.0.0.42:5762', wait_ready=True)


def send_attitude_target(self, roll_angle=0.0, pitch_angle=0.0,
                         yaw_angle=0.0, yaw_rate=10, thrust=0.5):
    msg = copter.message_factory.set_attitude_target_encode(
        0,
        0,                                         # target system
        0,                                         # target component
        0b00000000,                                # type mask: bit 1 is LSB
        to_quaternion(roll_angle, pitch_angle, yaw_angle),    # q
        0,                                         # body roll rate in radian
        0,                                         # body pitch rate in radian
        yaw_rate,                                  # body yaw rate in radian
        thrust)                                    # thrust
    copter.send_mavlink(msg)


def to_quaternion(self, roll=0.0, pitch=0.0, yaw=0.0):
    """Convert degrees to quaternions."""
    t0 = math.cos(yaw * 0.5)
    t1 = math.sin(yaw * 0.5)
    t2 = math.cos(roll * 0.5)
    t3 = math.sin(roll * 0.5)
    t4 = math.cos(pitch * 0.5)
    t5 = math.sin(pitch * 0.5)
    w = t0 * t2 * t4 + t1 * t3 * t5
    x = t0 * t3 * t4 - t1 * t2 * t5
    y = t0 * t2 * t5 + t1 * t3 * t4
    z = t1 * t2 * t4 - t0 * t3 * t5
    return [w, x, y, z]


while True:
    if(copter and copter.mode == dronekit.VehicleMode("GUIDED")):
        send_attitude_target(0,  # roll
                             0,  # pitch
                             0,  # yaw_angle
                             0,  # yaw_rate
                             0.5)  # thrust
        print("attitude command sent")
    time.sleep(0.1)
```
With this sample code constantly running, we took off in LOITER mode and then passed to GUIDED during the flight.

issue_title: Vehicle.commands.next returns last waypoint not next waypoint
issue_body:
The reported vehicle.commands.next value is numerically the waypoint that was just left rather than the waypoint that you're heading towards. This is incorrect

You can see the behaviour by observing mission_basic.py example (note we're reporting next waypoint as 0 (incorrect), then the system reports we've reached waypoint 1 (correctly)
```
Distance to waypoint (0): None
Distance to waypoint (0): None
Distance to waypoint (0): None
>>> Reached Command #1
Distance to waypoint (1): 7.44343078803
Distance to waypoint (1): 3.82302758257
```
As an aside, this causes the example to misreport the distance to the next waypoint (and rather report the distance from the last waypoint)

issue_title: Monitoring Loop timeouts/poll-rates are not what is naively expected.
issue_body:
Hi all,

This came up in last weeks meeting and I mentioned I wasn't sure if this was un-related. Now I think this is a quite serious (albeit separate) bug.

From what I can tell the poll rate for backends is being passed into ```timeoutMax``` in ```UpdateDict.addEntry```.
This doesn't appear to be used in executing code from the monitoring thread unless I've missed something?

In addition the use of base_poll_rate is out by a factor of 2 in HEAD so causes it to appear as if backend tasks are being polled every 1 sec instead of 2.
(This is an easily fixed typo)


After some investigating the monitoring system seems to run like this:

1. Infinite loop with break point is launched on construction of Monitoring loop.
2. Active backends and other queries are added to dictionary of actions in Monitoring system (internal set of queue instructions).
(potential bug, this appears to ALWAYS be populated once, regardless of if monitoring is supposed to be running)
3. Once running the main loop runs through the dictionary of actions and each action spawns a task on a queue system. This running through the dictionary is controlled by the 'base_poll_rate'.
4. Infinite loop keeps the queues system populated with 'tasks' from the dictionary of items

The queues system has it's own mechanics which is slightly difficult to disentangle from all of this.

1. A small thread-pool is setup on launch of ganga which cycles through the threading queues and runs tasks on various threads.
2. Actions are added to the queue from the main monitoring loop:
  * If the action isn't in this queue already it's added.
  * If the action is already in the queues give it the list of jobs of interest and return.
(no point in queueing if a friend is queueing for you and if the friend is busy, return and come back later)
3. Action is run on the queue system once it's reached it's turn. First thing it should do is get a thread lock on itself and remove itself from the queue. Whilst an action is executing it shouldn't allow for another action to cause it upset.


The polling times from the various backends appear to be used only for giving information when executing the command ```runMonitoring``` which is intended to perform a single pass of the infinite monitoring loop if the monitoring system is turned off.

 
 

__I have a few questions based upon this:__

1. Should we change the documentation to reflect the fact that these poll rates don't appear to effect the rate at which the backend is polled?
2. Should we allow the backends to be polled at different rates (We can easily add a conditional pause into the check backends function to sleep unless a certain length of time has passed)?
3. Do we need disk and credential polling here? Do they work?
  * Can we get rid of deprecated functionality
  * Can we simplify some of this code and collect a lot of related static functions into one place?
(There is code in the IBackend at the moment which should be in the monitoring system imo)
4. Should we just leave this all alone in favour of starting again with a new system based on something more modern?

After just inspecting the code I've spotted a few minor bugs which need addressing anyway and one of these relates to thread safety so I'm inclined to add these fixes into a PR and see what the performance of the monitoring under stress behaves like.

issue_title: Unpractical to pass a dictionary to CrystalNNFingerprint when aiming at augmenting with chemical information
issue_body:
Referring here to a comment by @computron in PR #250:

"In practice, I think people are going to have a hard time passing in the chem_info dict and find this to be a pain to use. While flexible, normally you have a dataset of 1000 compounds and don't want to manually construct a dictionary of all possible species to some value like electronegativity.

It might be better if this was able to easily work with an "AbstractData" class instead, since those already have a bunch of built-in dictionaries for various properties. Or, some easy way for the user to leverage those classes rather than construct the dictionary themselves.

Or, maybe instead AbstractData itself could implement a method that turns its properties into a dictionary. That way you could do PymatgenData.dict_output("X") that will turn all the PymatgenData electronegativities to a dictionary format."

issue_title: MockService blocks test execution if pact-python is installed in system Python's site-packages
issue_body:
Package versions:
* pact-python 0.14.0
* Python 3.6.3 from official docker image

When installing pact-python into system-Python's site-packages, which is quite common when using a docker container, the Mock-Service cannot be started if the Python tests in the docker-container are not executed with root permissions.

Basically I have a test that creates a `pact.Consumer` and then calls `consumer.pact.start_service()` to run the mock service within a pytest fixture. That's roughly equivalent to:

```python
import pact

consumer = pact.Consumer('consumer').has_pact_with(
    provider=pact.Provider('provider'),
    host_name='localhost',
    mock_port=1234,
)
consumer.pact.start_service()

# Execute test here.
# Tests want to access the running mock-service.

consumer.pact.stop_service()
```

However, during `consumer.service_start()` the MockService tries to modify its `Gemfile.lock` which is located at `/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pact/bin/pact/lib`.
When running the docker container without root permissions, this file cannot be modified.

pytest prints the error message when invoked with `-s` (no capture) flag:

```
There was an error while trying to write to Gemfile.lock. It is likely that 
you need to allow write permissions for the file at path: 
/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/pact/bin/pact/lib/vendor/Gemfile.lock
```

I posted a pull request for a related problem, which is that during the call to `consumer.pact.server_start()` the `Pact._wait_for_server_start` method retries a connection to the started MockService 15 times. With the implementation of `urllib3.utils.retry.Retry` with a `backoff_factor` of `0.1` that means 54 minutes wait time before the tests actually fail.

Thus it appears as if the tests would simply block. In combination with pytest's output capturing this was pretty hard to spot. :)

I don't really know Ruby, so I cannot tell why the `Gemfile.lock` has to be modified when starting the MockService. However, when installed as a vendored library of a global Python site-package, that doesn't work.
Maybe you could download the vendored ruby libs to a temporary / cache directory?

issue_title: manage.py set does not work for DateTime
issue_body:
### Describe the problem

Setting values using `manage.py` commands does not work for `DateTime` type.

### Steps to reproduce

`settings.py`:
```
CONSTANCE_CONFIG = {
    'foo': (datetime.datetime(1972, 11, 30, 1, 10, 0), 'help text'),
}
```
```
$ python manage.py constance list
foo	1972-11-30 01:10:00
$ python manage.py constance set foo '1972-11-30 01:10:00'
CommandError: Enter a list of values.
```
The `value` passed to `manage.py` first goes through `field.to_python` and then errors in `field.clean`, see [line 25](https://github.com/jazzband/django-constance/blob/8db90e808756f62941c025c6dc522156d7271db0/constance/management/commands/constance.py#L25). In the Django source tree (version `1.10.4`), `to_python` does not do anything, see [line 130](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.4/django/forms/fields.py#L130), while `clean` is checking if `value` is a `list` or a `tuple`, see [line 1015](https://github.com/django/django/blob/1.10.4/django/forms/fields.py#L1015). Since `value` is just a string, this code can't possibly work.

### System configuration

* Constance revision: 8db90e808756f62941c025c6dc522156d7271db0
* Django version: `1.10.4`
* Python version: `2.7.12`

I only noticed this because postgresql hit a constraint error on a table that does not currently exist in Django (we removed the app, but forgot to clean the tables).

I'm using `contrib.layers` (which is basically `tflean.layers`, right?) in multigpu setting (multiple "towers" in separate name scopes) and that leads to scope errors; using ordinary `tf.get_variable(..)` works just fine, though.

tensorflow 0.10.0
cuda 7.5

basic fully connected network to classify mnist: 
```
def build_fc_dnn():
    X = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 784])
    y = tf.placeholder(tf.float32, [None, 10])

    depth = 5
    
    last_layer = X
    argkw = dict(num_outputs=20, activation_fn=tf.nn.relu)
    for _ in range(depth):
        last_layer = tf.contrib.layers.fully_connected(last_layer, **argkw)
    logits = tf.contrib.layers.fully_connected(last_layer, num_outputs=10)
    
    loss = tf.nn.softmax_cross_entropy_with_logits(logits, y)
    y_train_eq_pred = tf.equal(tf.argmax(y,1), tf.argmax(logits,1))
    acc = tf.reduce_mean(tf.cast(y_train_eq_pred, tf.float32))
    
    return X, y, loss, acc

issue_title: Don't use absolute paths in HTML
issue_body:
Hi,
Right now, the HTML returned from the server has elements like 

```html
<link rel="stylesheet" href=http://localhost:8097/static/css/bootstrap.min.css?v=ec3bb52a00e176a7181d454dffaea219>
```

with the `href` containing an absolute path. This makes it hard to use Visdom in a reverse-proxy setting since the client will try to request those absolute URLs, which in general won't be accessible to them. If the paths were relative, however (like  `/static/css/bootstrap.min.css?v=ec3bb52a00e176a7181d454dffaea219`), everything would automatically work in the proxy setting.

issue_title: csvgrep does not return results if more than one column is specified
issue_body:
Say you use a csv file `tips.csv` (test file from the `pandas` package) which starts like

    total_bill,tip,sex,smoker,day,time,size
    16.99,1.01,Female,No,Sun,Dinner,2
    10.34,1.66,Male,No,Sun,Dinner,3
    21.01,3.5,Male,No,Sun,Dinner,3
    23.68,3.31,Male,No,Sun,Dinner,2
    24.59,3.61,Female,No,Sun,Dinner,4
    25.29,4.71,Male,No,Sun,Dinner,4

With this if you issue on the command line

   $ csvgrep -c tip -m 3.31 tips.csv 

you get as expected 

    total_bill,tip,sex,smoker,day,time,size
    23.68,3.31,Male,No,Sun,Dinner,2

but if you search through more columns,e.g.:

   $ csvgrep -c tip,sex -m 3.31 tips.csv 

you get  no data row back:

    total_bill,tip,sex,smoker,day,time,size

This is wrong. csvgrep should return the same data row
`23.68,3.31,Male,No,Sun,Dinner,2`. The same happens when specifying
the columns by index (`-c 2` works, but e.g. `-c 1,2` does not).

Additionally it would be great to have an easy way to tell csvgrep to search all columns.

<!--  LocalWords:  csvgrep csv
 -->

issue_title: Update the README
No option to turn off pipeline estimators with None in params
issue_body:
#### Description
Unlike the regular [scikit-learn Pipeline object](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/pipeline.html) there is no option to turn off an estimator in the parameter grid (with None). This is extremely useful for model selection using (Gridsearch) crossvalidation.

Either the imblearn Pipeline object is to be changed, or the imblearn transformers as to be compatible with sklearn's Pipeline.

#### Steps/Code to Reproduce
```
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.models import svm
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE

estimators = [('resample', SMOTE()), ('classify', svm.SVC())]
pipe = Pipeline(estimators)
param_grid = {'resample': [None, SMOTE()],}

gridsearch = GridSearch(pipe, param_grid)

gridsearch.fit(X, y)
```

#### Expected Results
Regular old gridsearch starts with None estimator enabled

#### Actual Results
Error saying None is not an estimator object.

#### Versions
Linux-4.4.0-59-generic-x86_64-with-Ubuntu-16.04-xenial
('Python', '2.7.12 (default, Jul  1 2016, 15:12:24) \n[GCC 5.4.0 20160609]')
('NumPy', '1.12.0')
('SciPy', '0.18.1')

('Imbalanced-Learn', '0.2.1')

issue_title: Create bindings to an existing C++ object
Cannot call embedded function from within class function
issue_body:
I've written a c++ binding that allows me to post a simple message such that:
`
import foo
foo.func("message")
`


when called in python will post "message".

However, if I call the same embedded function from within a class method such as:
`
import foo

class bar(object):
  def func2(self):
    foo.func("message")

`

I will get an exception error_already_set. This occurs if I call the function from python or from c++.
 I'm not sure if this is a pybind11 bug, or if I just missed something.

issue_title: Interactive Apache domain order not respected
issue_body:
If you don't provide any domains on the command line or in a config file when using the Apache plugin, it attempts to suggest domain names for you like so:
```
Which names would you like to activate HTTPS for?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1: other.example.com
2: another.example.com
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Select the appropriate numbers separated by commas and/or spaces (Enter 'c' to
cancel):
```
At this prompt, however, the order you input your domain names does not affect the common name. The common name that is used is the selected domain name that comes first in the list.

We should change this so the domain order can be used to select the common name like at all other prompts in the client.
